EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Prolonged hot and humid conditions coupled with dry mid-levels in the atmosphere, led to extreme amounts of mixed-layer convective available potential energy (MLCAPE). The existence of deep layer shear allowed for numerous strong updrafts to develop across East Central MN with the primary hazard being hail in the afternoon hours. Later that evening in Southwestern MN, a surface boundary would converge on a cold pool which then would produce a line of thunderstorms capable of producing damaging wind gusts.||Hail measurements ranged between penny (0.75 inch) to Hen-Egg (2.0 inch) size. The highest measured hail was reported in the city of Morgan, MN or 13 miles SE of Redwood Falls located in Redwood County. ||Strong winds uprooted trees, broke large tree branches across much of the region affected by these storms. The areas with the most damage occurred in Chisago County as well as a line from Nicollet to Waseca Counties. The strongest gust (57 mph) was recorded at 11:56 PM at the Mankato Regional Airport.

Multiple bands of accumulating snow. ||A long duration winter storm produced multiple bands of widespread significant snowfall across Minnesota, with persistent northwest winds developing as the storm exited the region. Snow began to fall on the morning of February 21st and persisted in waves through the morning of February 23rd. Wind gust observations were in the low to mid 30 mph range. Overall snow accumulations provided by a combination of trained spotters and the CoCoRaHS observers ranged from 8-16 inches across the region, with a few localized higher amounts. ||Some of the highest snowfall totals across the area include:||16.8 inches near Victoria in Carver County.|16.6 inches near Prior Lake in Scott County.|16.5 inches near Danube in Renville County.|16.5 inches near Faribault in Rice County.|16.1 inches near New Prague in Le Sueur County.|16.0 inches near Prinsburg in Kandiyohi County.|15.9 inches near Cambridge in Isanti County.|15.7 inches near Waseca in Waseca County.|15.4 inches near Stark in Chisago County.|15.3 inches near Corvuso in Meeker County.|15.1 inches near Luxemburg in Stearns County.|15.0 inches near Little Canada in Ramsey County.|15.0 inches near South St Paul in Dakota County.|14.6 inches near New Ulm in Brown County.|14.5 inches near Bethel in Anoka County.|14.2 inches near Edina in Hennepin County.|14.0 inches near Mora in Mille Lacs County.|12.2 inches near Morris in Stevens County.|11.3 inches near Albert Lea in Freeborn County.|11.6 inches near Winnebago in Faribault County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple bands of snow moved across the county. Sustained winds increased out of the northeast near 20-25 mph with gusts near 30-40 mph per the Mankato Airport ASOS observations. Across the county, Interpolated Snowfall Analysis using the National Gridded Snowfall Analysis (NGSA) map detailed that at least 4-8 inches fell in 24 hrs, 4-12 inches in 48 hrs making an overall total range of 8-18 inches. CoCoRaHS and Trained Spotter observations helped verify the NGSA by showing highest snowfall observations located in the northern region of the county with 10-16 measured inches. The highest reported measurement was in 16.1 inches in New Prague, MN.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper-level wave moving through southwest flow aloft, acting on an environment with 1000 to 1500 J/kg of MUCAPE in the vicinity of a surface trough, resulted in scattered thunderstorms across far southeast South Dakota, far northeast Nebraska, northwest Iowa, and southwest Minnesota during the evening of April 19th. The strongest storms produced severe hail ranging from 1.0 to 1.5 inches in diameter.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail 0.75 inch in diameter fell in Balaton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure deepened as it moved from eastern Colorado on April 4 to central Minnesota on April 5. Slightly warmer and drier air aloft east of the upper-level low track kept precipitation type as mainly light freezing rain and freezing drizzle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Periods of freezing drizzle, mixed briefly with snow, brought a light glaze of ice accumulations around Lake Wilson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms pushed through NW MN and some in NE ND, but the strongest storms were in Minnesota. Wind gusts around 60 MPH and hail were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was a 61 mph wind gust reported at the Eldred NDAWN site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms dropped quarter sized hail in Lake of the Woods county in Northern Minnesota and Northeastern North Dakota in the afternoon and evening of June 27th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reports quarter sized hail 9 miles east southeast of Baudette.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Beltrami county emergency manager reports 2.1 inches of rain from personal rain gauge in just under an hour. Storm drains are backing up in town, with flash flooding occurring.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface low pressure system tracked across the North Dakota/South Dakota border creating an environment supporting the threat for low top supercells in Southeastern North Dakota into adjacent West Central Minnesota. Several low-top supercells developed and tracked along portions of the Minnesota Red River Valley. Portions of Norman, Polk, Becker, and Mahnomen counties saw multiple tornadoes. Large hail and damaging winds accompanied some of these storms, and flash flooding in some locations as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to quarter-sized and 40 to 50 MPH winds estimated at Big Timber Lodge. Time estimated via radar.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado touched down south of Mahnomen at 4:15 PM. The highest damage it caused was rated as an EF-2. Damage dollar amounts are estimated.

Overall snow accumulations provided by a combination of trained spotters and the CoCoRaHS observers ranged from 8-16 inches across the region, with a few localized higher amounts. ||Some of the highest snowfall totals across the area include:||16.8 inches near Victoria in Carver County.|16.6 inches near Prior Lake in Scott County.|16.5 inches near Danube in Renville County.|16.5 inches near Faribault in Rice County.|16.1 inches near New Prague in Le Sueur County.|16.0 inches near Prinsburg in Kandiyohi County.|15.9 inches near Cambridge in Isanti County.|15.7 inches near Waseca in Waseca County.|15.4 inches near Stark in Chisago County.|15.3 inches near Corvuso in Meeker County.|15.1 inches near Luxemburg in Stearns County.|15.0 inches near Little Canada in Ramsey County.|15.0 inches near South St Paul in Dakota County.|14.6 inches near New Ulm in Brown County.|14.5 inches near Bethel in Anoka County.|14.2 inches near Edina in Hennepin County.|14.0 inches near Mora in Mille Lacs County.|12.2 inches near Morris in Stevens County.|11.3 inches near Albert Lea in Freeborn County.|11.6 inches near Winnebago in Faribault County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple bands of snow moved across the county. Sustained winds increased out of the northeast near 10-15 mph with gusts near 25 mph per Rush City ASOS observations. Across the county, Interpolated Snowfall Analysis using the National Gridded Snowfall Analysis (NGSA) map detailed that at least 1-4 inches fell in 24 hrs, 6-12 inches in 48 hrs making an overall total range of 7-15 inches. CoCoRaHS and Trained Spotter observations helped verify the NGSA by showing highest snowfall observations located in the central region of the county with 11-16 measured inches. The highest reported measurement was 15.9 inches in North Branch, MN.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving system brought some freezing rain to portions of southeast Minnesota from January 2nd into the 3rd. Ice accumulations around a quarter of an inch were reported in Byron (Olmsted County), Rochester (Olmsted County) and near Wilson (Winona County). The ice quickly melted on the 3rd as temperatures rose above freezing.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters reported around a quarter inch of ice accumulation across portions of Winona County from freezing rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving area of low pressure moved into the western Great Lakes region on the 3rd and then lingered there through the 4th. This brought an area of snowfall to the Lake Mille Lacs area eastward in northeastern Minnesota. Snowfall totals were in the 6 to 12 inch range in this area with higher amounts to just over 18 inches in Carlton County due to lake and terrain enhancement.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals across the southern portion of the county were in the 6 to 10 inch range. The highest report was 11.5 inches near Isle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold high pressure remained in place across the Northland for the night of the 29th into the 30s. Winds were much lighter this night with many areas seeing calm winds. As air temperatures dropped once again into the 20s and 30s below zero F, it didn't take much wind to generate dangerous wind chills. As the sun began to rise, winds increased enought to create wind chills in the 40s below zero F with Ash Lake reaching -47F.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills dropped as low as -41F at International Falls for around 3 hours around sunrise on the 30th.

Overall snow accumulations provided by a combination of trained spotters and the CoCoRaHS observers ranged from 8-16 inches across the region, with a few localized higher amounts. ||Some of the highest snowfall totals across the area include:||16.8 inches near Victoria in Carver County.|16.6 inches near Prior Lake in Scott County.|16.5 inches near Danube in Renville County.|16.5 inches near Faribault in Rice County.|16.1 inches near New Prague in Le Sueur County.|16.0 inches near Prinsburg in Kandiyohi County.|15.9 inches near Cambridge in Isanti County.|15.7 inches near Waseca in Waseca County.|15.4 inches near Stark in Chisago County.|15.3 inches near Corvuso in Meeker County.|15.1 inches near Luxemburg in Stearns County.|15.0 inches near Little Canada in Ramsey County.|15.0 inches near South St Paul in Dakota County.|14.6 inches near New Ulm in Brown County.|14.5 inches near Bethel in Anoka County.|14.2 inches near Edina in Hennepin County.|14.0 inches near Mora in Mille Lacs County.|12.2 inches near Morris in Stevens County.|11.3 inches near Albert Lea in Freeborn County.|11.6 inches near Winnebago in Faribault County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple bands of snow moved across the county. Sustained winds increased out of the northeast near 20-25 mph with gusts near 30-35 mph per the Waseca Airport AWOS observations. Across the county, Interpolated Snowfall Analysis using the National Gridded Snowfall Analysis (NGSA) map detailed that at least 6-8 inches fell in 24 hrs, 4-6 inches in 48 hrs making an overall total range of 6-18 inches. CoCoRaHS and Trained Spotter observations helped verify the NGSA by showing highest snowfall observations located in the northern region of the county with 10-13 measured inches. The highest reported measurement was 15.0 inches in Waseca, MN.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving system brought some freezing rain to portions of southeast Minnesota from January 2nd into the 3rd. Ice accumulations around a quarter of an inch were reported in Byron (Olmsted County), Rochester (Olmsted County) and near Wilson (Winona County). The ice quickly melted on the 3rd as temperatures rose above freezing.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Volunteer observers reported around a quarter inch of ice accumulation across Olmsted County from freezing rain. After the freezing rain, city of Rochester officials closed a street and sidewalks in downtown for safety reason due to falling ice from buildings.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level wave lifted out of the southwestern U.S. and closed off moving across Iowa by early on January 19. A band of snow developed within the leading warm advection zone from northern Nebraska to northern Iowa by late afternoon on January 18, and with unstable conditions near the frontal axis, the band evolved into a more narrow zone of intense snowfall into the overnight hours. Dry air poised to the north created a sharp gradient to snowfall on the north side of the band across southwest Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 5.0 inches 2 miles northeast of Lakefield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On January 03 a band of heavy snow lifted up from the south and produced 1 to 2 inches per hour. Schools closed in advance of this system, and there were 3 ground stops at the MSP airport: 1221-1515 CST (for snow), 1701-1812 CST (for freezing drizzle), 1902-2030 CST (for freezing drizzle). This was part 1 of a two-part system. The second part is entered as a separate episode. Below are a few snowfall reports from Part 1 of the system:||6.4 inches in Waconia.|7 inches near Montrose.|8.0 inches in Canby.|8.5 inches near Willmar.|7 inches near Granite Falls.|8.5 inches near Comfrey.|9 inches near Clara City.||Then there was a break with some freezing drizzle in the middle. Part 2 was another area of snow developed and pivoted across eastern Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A heavy band of snow, with rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour caused significant travel impacts across the region as it moved through. Snowfall totals were 4 to 7 inches across the region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long-duration winter storm developed across southwest Minnesota on February 21, with waves of heavier snowfall across the region as winds increased to a gusty 30 to 50 mph. Blizzard conditions developed during the afternoon of February 22, with strong winds lingering into the midday hours of February 23. During the event, widespread blowing and drifting snowfall led to the closure of Interstate 90, as well as Interstate 29 north of Sioux Falls. Roads were closed and travel was not recommended in numerous counties, with schools and county/state offices closed due to the hazardous conditions. Total snowfall varied 10 to 18 inches, with locally as much as 22 inches around Tracy.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over the two-day period, snowfall accumulated to 13.9 inches at Windom.  Whiteout conditions became widespread, especially in rural areas, due to winds which peaked at 39 mph near Jeffers and Mountain Lake, and 37 mph at Windom.

During the event, widespread blowing and drifting snowfall led to the closure of Interstate 90, as well as Interstate 29 north of Sioux Falls. Roads were closed and travel was not recommended in numerous counties, with schools and county/state offices closed due to the hazardous conditions. Total snowfall varied 10 to 18 inches, with locally as much as 22 inches around Tracy.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over the two-day period, snowfall accumulated to 22.0 inches at Tracy, 18.0 inches at Minneota, and 15.0 inches at Marshall. Whiteout conditions became widespread, especially in rural areas, due to winds which peaked at 45 mph at Tracy, Taunton, and near Lake Benton, and 41 mph at Marshall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure intensified and moved from the Central Plains into Minnesota. Initially, warm thermal profiles allowed for mixed precipitation or all rain. However, as winds shifted to the northwest, colder air rapidly changed precipitation over to snow. While additional accumulation of snow was fairly light (up to 1 to 3 inches in areas of greatest snowfall along an axis from Tyndall to Salem to Minneota), northwest winds were bolstered to a gusty 40 to 60 mph and resulted in widespread blowing snow along with spotty blizzard conditions near the Buffalo Ridge. Travel was not recommended during the overnight and early morning hours across parts of southwest Minnesota and east central South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Northwest wind reached gusts to 49 mph at the Minnesota Road Weather Information System near Magnolia and 43 mph at the Automated Weather Observing System site KLYV near Luverne. Despite only a half inch of snowfall near Edgerton, winds whipped up considerable blowing snow with visibility of a mile or less at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow moved across southeast Minnesota from the evening of January 18th into the morning of January 19th. Snowfall amounts ranged from 4 to 8 inches across much of the area. The highest reported totals were 8.0 inches in Rochester (Olmsted County) and Theilman (Wabasha County) as well as 6.5 inches in Rushford (Fillmore County) and Winona (Winona County). Many schools across the area canceled classes or had a late start on January 19th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Volunteer snow observers reported that 4 to 6 inches of snow fell across Dodge County. The highest reported total was 6.3 inches in Kasson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Periods of heavy snowfall buried much of southwest Minnesota on January 2-3. The first band of heavy snow developed on the morning of January 2, narrowing as it lifted north and locked in along a Dell Rapids to Slayton line. This band broadened with lesser rates through the night, but convective development resulted in a secondary period of heavier snowfall (with rates up to 2 to 3 inches per hour) within the deformation zone northwest of the upper level low across southeast South Dakota and southwest Minnesota during the early morning to midday hours on January 3. Areas that received both heavy bands saw two-day totals of 12 to 20 inches from Luverne to Tracy and Slayton. Roads were closed or impassable during the height of the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snowfall occurred with a two-day total of 8.5 inches 8 miles southeast of Hendricks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Periods of heavy snowfall buried much of southwest Minnesota on January 2-3. The first band of heavy snow developed on the morning of January 2, narrowing as it lifted north and locked in along a Dell Rapids to Slayton line. This band broadened with lesser rates through the night, but convective development resulted in a secondary period of heavier snowfall (with rates up to 2 to 3 inches per hour) within the deformation zone northwest of the upper level low across southeast South Dakota and southwest Minnesota during the early morning to midday hours on January 3. Areas that received both heavy bands saw two-day totals of 12 to 20 inches from Luverne to Tracy and Slayton. Roads were closed or impassable during the height of the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snowfall occurred with a two-day total of 16.0 inches at Tracy, 13.0 inches at Ghent, and 10.0 inches at Minneota.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level wave lifted out of the southwestern U.S. and closed off moving across Iowa by early on January 19. A band of snow developed within the leading warm advection zone from northern Nebraska to northern Iowa by late afternoon on January 18, and with unstable conditions near the frontal axis, the band evolved into a more narrow zone of intense snowfall into the overnight hours. Dry air poised to the north created a sharp gradient to snowfall on the north side of the band across southwest Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 5.8 inches in Hills.

Most locations picked up 4 to 6 inches of snow, with a few spots reporting around 7 inches.||Below are a few of the noteworthy reports:|7.0 inches near Owatonna in Steele County.|5.7 inches near Faribault in Rice County.|5.9 inches near Waseca in Waseca County.|6.2 inches near Nerstrand in Goodhue County.|5.5 inches near Blue Earth in Faribault County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A band of heavy snow lifted up from Iowa and produced 3 to 5 inches across the county. Most of the snow fell between midnight and 6 AM on the 19th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure intensified and moved from the Central Plains into Minnesota. Initially, warm thermal profiles allowed for mixed precipitation or all rain. However, as winds shifted to the northwest, colder air rapidly changed precipitation over to snow. While additional accumulation of snow was fairly light (up to 1 to 3 inches in areas of greatest snowfall along an axis from Tyndall to Salem to Minneota), northwest winds were bolstered to a gusty 40 to 60 mph and resulted in widespread blowing snow along with spotty blizzard conditions near the Buffalo Ridge. Travel was not recommended during the overnight and early morning hours across parts of southwest Minnesota and east central South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Northwest winds were sustained from 30 to 35 mph, with gusts as high as 50 mph at the Minnesota Road Weather Information System site MN008 near Lakefield, and 49 mph at the Automated Weather Observing System site KMJQ near Jackson.  While there was little additional snowfall, some patchy blowing snow reduced visibility briefly to around a mile at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band of rain and freezing rain lifted up across southern Minnesota on the morning of January 03, 2023. There were several pictures of icing on social media. Call to dispatchers verified a few counties in the ice storm warning. Those that didn't have significant impacts were a couple degrees away from a disaster according to one Emergency Manager.||Below are a couple of the noteworthy reports:|Blue Earth County - a couple calls of powerlines iced and leaning. A CoCoRAHs observer measured 0.25 of ice on upwind side of rain gage, and another mentioned quarter inch of sleet/ice.|Faribault County - A few power outages and slippery roads.|Martin County - A few power outages, and at least 1/4 inch of ice on a fence.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Calls to dispatch centers revealed slippery roads and a few power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On January 03 a band of heavy snow lifted up from the south and produced 1 to 2 inches per hour. Schools closed in advance of this system, and there were 3 ground stops at the MSP airport: 1221-1515 CST (for snow), 1701-1812 CST (for freezing drizzle), 1902-2030 CST (for freezing drizzle). This was part 1 of a two-part system. The second part is entered as a separate episode. Below are a few snowfall reports from Part 1 of the system:||6.4 inches in Waconia.|7 inches near Montrose.|8.0 inches in Canby.|8.5 inches near Willmar.|7 inches near Granite Falls.|8.5 inches near Comfrey.|9 inches near Clara City.||Then there was a break with some freezing drizzle in the middle. Part 2 was another area of snow developed and pivoted across eastern Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A heavy band of snow, with rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour caused significant travel impacts across the region as it moved through. Snowfall totals were 4 to 7 inches across the region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long-duration winter storm developed across southwest Minnesota on February 21, with waves of heavier snowfall across the region as winds increased to a gusty 30 to 50 mph. Blizzard conditions developed during the afternoon of February 22, with strong winds lingering into the midday hours of February 23. During the event, widespread blowing and drifting snowfall led to the closure of Interstate 90, as well as Interstate 29 north of Sioux Falls. Roads were closed and travel was not recommended in numerous counties, with schools and county/state offices closed due to the hazardous conditions. Total snowfall varied 10 to 18 inches, with locally as much as 22 inches around Tracy.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over the two-day period, snowfall accumulated to 10.5 inches at Hills.  Whiteout conditions became widespread, especially in rural areas, due to winds which peaked at 40 mph near Magnolia and 39 mph at Luverne.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On January 03 a band of heavy snow lifted up from the south and produced 1 to 2 inches per hour. This was followed by some freezing drizzle in the middle, and then another area of snow developed and pivoted across eastern Minnesota and western Wisconsin the night of the 03 into the 04. This produced 5 to 8 more inches of snow across the region, specifically the Twin Cities metro area and northern I-35 corridor.||Below are some noteworthy snow totals for the entire event:|14.2 inches near Prior Lake in Scott County.|12.5 inches near Woodbury in Washington County.|10.9 inches near Mahtomedi in Washington County.|10.9 inches near Ham Lake in Anoka County.|10.5 inches near Lindstrom in Chisago County.|10.5 inches near Victoria in Carver County.|10.5 inches near Mora in Kanabec County.|12.2 inches near Maplewood in Ramsey County.|11.0 inches near Minneapolis in Hennepin County.|12.0 inches near Rosemount in Dakota County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were a few hours of freezing drizzle which led to a glazing of ice, followed by another widespread 5 to 7 inches of snow. Total snowfall amounts between Jan 03-04 were near a foot, with locally higher amounts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved across Kansas, Iowa, and into Minnesota from January 2nd through the 4th. The storm system spread accumulating snowfall, mainly to the southern half of Big Stone County. The highest snowfall amount was 6.5 inches near the Ortonville Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of light freezing rain developed across central and eastern Minnesota overnight on  January 10th through the morning of January 11th. Most locations saw just a glazing of ice, but this was enough to cause travel impacts. This was a true freezing rain event, with a stout warm nose aloft and surface temperatures in the 20s. The quantitative precipitation forecast (total amount) was only a few hundredths, which is exactly what fell across the region. A Winter Weather Advisory was issued for this event. ||The Minnesota State Patrol tweeted that statewide there were 120 crashes, and 116 reported vehicle spin outs with 19 jackknifed semis. Metro transit pulled the buses off the roads at 5AM.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: MNDOT reported light glazing of ice on roads.

This produced 5 to 8 more inches of snow across the region, specifically the Twin Cities metro area and northern I-35 corridor.||Below are some noteworthy snow totals for the entire event:|14.2 inches near Prior Lake in Scott County.|12.5 inches near Woodbury in Washington County.|10.9 inches near Mahtomedi in Washington County.|10.9 inches near Ham Lake in Anoka County.|10.5 inches near Lindstrom in Chisago County.|10.5 inches near Victoria in Carver County.|10.5 inches near Mora in Kanabec County.|12.2 inches near Maplewood in Ramsey County.|11.0 inches near Minneapolis in Hennepin County.|12.0 inches near Rosemount in Dakota County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were a few hours of freezing drizzle which led to a glazing of ice, followed by another widespread 5 to 7 inches of snow. MSP airport measured 6.2 between midnight and 12pm. Total snowfall amounts between Jan 03-04 were near a foot, with locally higher amounts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of light freezing rain developed across central and eastern Minnesota overnight on  January 10th through the morning of January 11th. Most locations saw just a glazing of ice, but this was enough to cause travel impacts. This was a true freezing rain event, with a stout warm nose aloft and surface temperatures in the 20s. The quantitative precipitation forecast (total amount) was only a few hundredths, which is exactly what fell across the region. A Winter Weather Advisory was issued for this event. ||The Minnesota State Patrol tweeted that statewide there were 120 crashes, and 116 reported vehicle spin outs with 19 jackknifed semis. Metro transit pulled the buses off the roads at 5AM.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: MNDOT reported light glazing of ice on roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure intensified and moved from the Central Plains into Minnesota. Initially, warm thermal profiles allowed for mixed precipitation or all rain. However, as winds shifted to the northwest, colder air rapidly changed precipitation over to snow. While additional accumulation of snow was fairly light (up to 1 to 3 inches in areas of greatest snowfall along an axis from Tyndall to Salem to Minneota), northwest winds were bolstered to a gusty 40 to 60 mph and resulted in widespread blowing snow along with spotty blizzard conditions near the Buffalo Ridge. Travel was not recommended during the overnight and early morning hours across parts of southwest Minnesota and east central South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Northwest wind reached gusts to 56 mph at the Minnesota Road Weather Information System site near Jeffers and 53 mph near Mountain Lake, with 48 mph at the Automated Weather Observing System site KMWM near Windom. Despite less than a half inch of new snowfall at Windom, winds whipped up considerable blowing snow with visibility of a half mile or less at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A clipper low pressure system tracked across North Dakota into western Minnesota through the daytime of February 20th. It generated light snowfall of a half inch or less across west central Minnesota, and then strong winds. Winds of 40 to 50 mph were widespread in west central Minnesota with a peak wind of 51 mph measured at a personal weather station on Lake Traverse. The Wheaton municipal airport gusted to 41 mph.||Blowing and drifting snow across icy roads had a significant impact on travel and the sheriff's office in Wheaton reported numerous stranded vehicles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Traverse County sheriff's office reported multiple stranded vehicles with blowing snow and icy roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Areas of severe (D2) and extreme (D3) drought in southeast Minnesota along the Iowa border remained unchanged through the month of November. While October monthly rainfall totals were near or slightly above normal, the long-standing abnormally dry conditions comprised of total rainfall of 15 to 20 inches from April 1 to October 31st was 9 to 12 inches below normal. During November, only 1 to 3 days of measurable precipitation provided under 0.25 inches of total rainfall, as much as 2 inches below the monthly normal. While the growing season was all but over, multiple days of above normal temperatures, 10 to 25 degrees above normal, inhibited the ground from freezing. The drought remained unchanged through November as southern Mower, Fillmore, and Houston counties experienced persistent severe drought (D2), with extreme drought (D3) conditions right along the Iowa border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) to extreme (D3) drought persisted across the southern half of Houston County through November. The combination of total monthly precipitation near 0.25 inches, near 2 inches below normal, and anomalously warm temperatures for several days according  resulted in 30% below normal topsoil moisture across Houston County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved quickly across South Dakota and rapidly deepened as it tracked into Minnesota and the Western Great Lakes region. An initial rain/snow mix on the morning of Tuesday, February 14th transitioned to all snow around 7pm. Snow proceeded to continue through the early morning hours of Wednesday, February 15, with accumulations topping out at 3 inches in Wheaton, 2.6 inches southeast of Artichoke Lake and 2 inches in Ortonville. The main impact of the system was the winds however. Peak winds gusts of 60 mph were measured 4 miles southeast of Ortonville, 59 mph was recorded 3 miles southwest of Graceville, and the Wheaton municipal airport observed a peak gust of 56 mph.  ||The combination of high winds and fresh snow resulted in blizzard conditions. The Department of Transportation advised no travel for both Big Stone and Traverse counties by 11pm February 14th, and it wasn't lifted until late in the morning on February 15th. The sheriff in Big Stone County responded to at least 6 stranded vehicles, while the sheriff in Wheaton responded to at least 4 stranded vehicles in Traverse County. Power was knocked out to over 700 customers across Big Stone County during the morning hours and wasn't restored until around noon on the 15th. A Verizon outage occurred on the morning of the 15th as well, which limited the ability of customers to contact power companies to register these outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intermittent light snow from Saturday November 25th through Sunday November 26th resulted in slippery road conditions for the busy Thanksgiving holiday travel weekend. Minnesota Department of Transportation and broadcast media reported many slide offs and minor accidents across southeast Minnesota. While snowfall rates remained under 0.1 inches per hour for much of the weekend, a scattered increase to 0.25 inches per hour Sunday evening resulted in a temporary decrease in visibilities.  The longer duration resulted in a widespread 0.5 to 1.5 inches of overall accumulations by Sunday night. Highest snowfall reports were 2.0 inches near Stewartville (Olmsted County), and 1.5 inches near Caledonia (Houston County) and in Minnesota City (Winona County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Volunteer observers reported 0.5 to 1.0 inches of snowfall, highest recorded near Austin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow and strong winds developed across western Minnesota in association with a classic Colorado Low type storm system around mid-day Wednesday, February 22, and snow and gusty winds lingered into the early morning hours of Thursday, February 23rd. The highest total snowfall totals included 15 inches 2 miles east southeast of Artichoke, 13 inches in Ortonville, and 11.5 inches in Wheaton. Many locations experienced peak gusts between 30 and 40 mph.

A strong spring storm at the end of March provided over 1.5 of rainfall in parts of southeastern Minnesota on frozen ground. The rapid runoff resulted in rises along the Whitewater River in Winona and Olmsted counties at the start of April. While most sites did not reach flood stage, minor flooding was realized at Beaver (Winona County) with a crest of 700.17 ft above sea level, which is 1.17 ft over flood stage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong spring storm at the end of March provided over 1.5 of rainfall in parts of southeastern Minnesota, falling on frozen ground. The resultant rapid runoff resulted in sites along the Whitewater River in Winona and Olmsted counties to quickly rise above flood stage on April 1st. Minor flooding was recorded at Beaver where a crest of 700.17 ft above sea level was recorded, 1.17 ft over flood stage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The spring snowmelt flooding began in late April 2023 and persisted into the early portions of May for some locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pembina - Crest of 46.68 ft occurred on 5/4 (Noted in May 2023 storm data).

Initially, rain and thunderstorms spread through southern Minnesota before switching over to snow during the early morning hours of Sunday, April 16th. Heaviest snowfall occurred in southeast Minnesota during the late morning of Sunday, April 16th, where recorded snowfall totals were 12.0 near La Crescent (Houston County) and 10.5 near Pickwick (Winona County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observers reported 1 to 3 inches of snow fell in parts of Dodge County. The slippery conditions resulted in a motor vehicle losing control and having an accident just after midnight Monday April 17th. The driver and the passenger were hospitalized with non-life threatening injuries.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds chills fell to -42F at the International Falls airport during the early morning hours of the 1st.

Air temperatures fell into the 20s and 30s below zero F and light winds led to wind chills in the 40s and 50s F below zero. The coldest wind chill was -52F near Birchdale in Koochiching County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills dropped into the 40s and 50s below zero across the county with a coldest reading of -52F near Birchdale.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band of snowfall set up across parts of the Northland during the overnight hours of the 21st into the morning of the 22nd. Dry air near the surface initially limited snowfall but snow became heavier later in the night and began accumulating. Snowfall totals were generally in the 2 to 5 inch range across the southern portion of the Northland along with some embedded areas of higher snowfall. Higher amounts of 6 to 9 inches were reported with the highest total of 9 inches reported near Pine Center.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals of 1 to 4 inches were reported across southern Cass County with a high report of 6.1 inches near Pillager.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving storm system produced several days of snow across southeast Minnesota. The snow began during the afternoon of February 21st and continued on and off into the morning of the 23rd. Two heavier snow bands, the first occurring during the afternoon of the 21st and the second during the afternoon of the 22nd, resulted in widespread heavy snowfall. The highest accumulations of 11 to 14 inches over the three days were reported from Fillmore to Wabasha counties. The Governor of Minnesota declared a peacetime emergency and authorized the National Guard to aid any drivers stranded by winter weather. Numerous schools canceled classes for the 22nd while some businesses closed early.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Volunteer snow observers reported that a total of 10 to 12 inches of snow fell across Fillmore County. An initial band of snow on the 21st provided 1 to 2 inches with an additional 8 to 10 inches falling late on the 22nd into the 23rd. The highest reported total was 11.5 inches in Spring Valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early in the morning of November 29th a school bus collided with a sedan in Hillsdale Township at the intersection of Minnesota State Highway 14 and Ridge View Road. According to nearby automated weather observation stations, surface temperatures were around 20 degrees with relative humidity of 90 to 100% through the early morning. Minnesota State Patrol reported snow or ice covered road ways. The driver of the sedan sustained life-threatening injuries. Minnesota State Highway 14 was closed between Winona and Stockton for two hours as a result.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minnesota State Patrol reported a collision between a school bus and a sedan during the early morning hours of November 29th at the intersection of Minnesota State Highway 14 and Ridge View Road. The State Patrol also reported ice or snow covered roadways at the time and life threatening injuries sustained by the driver of the sedan. Nearby weather observation stations reported relative humidity values of 90 to 100% and surface temperatures of 20 to 25 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deeper than average snow pack from northern Minnesota into northern Wisconsin underwent a rapid melt through much of April from unseasonably warm temperatures combined with multiple rain events. This resulted in increased water levels and significant flooding for the entire length of the local Mississippi River, with most sites climbing to their third to fifth highest crests on record. Initial flood warnings were issued in mid April with peak stages realized in late April. The highest above flood stage was Winona Dam at 5.81 ft over flood stage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant upstream runoff from the combination of rainfall and rapidly melting snow pushed the Mississippi River at Winona into flood stage on April 16th. A crest of 18.81 ft occurred on April 26th, 5.81 ft over flood stage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band of snowfall set up across parts of the Northland during the overnight hours of the 21st into the morning of the 22nd. Dry air near the surface initially limited snowfall but snow became heavier later in the night and began accumulating. Snowfall totals were generally in the 2 to 5 inch range across the southern portion of the Northland along with some embedded areas of higher snowfall. Higher amounts of 6 to 9 inches were reported with the highest total of 9 inches reported near Pine Center.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals of 2 to 6 inches were reported across the area with a high report of 8 inches near Wrenshall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving storm system produced several days of snow across southeast Minnesota. The snow began during the afternoon of February 21st and continued on and off into the morning of the 23rd. Two heavier snow bands, the first occurring during the afternoon of the 21st and the second during the afternoon of the 22nd, resulted in widespread heavy snowfall. The highest accumulations of 11 to 14 inches over the three days were reported from Fillmore to Wabasha counties. The Governor of Minnesota declared a peacetime emergency and authorized the National Guard to aid any drivers stranded by winter weather. Numerous schools canceled classes for the 22nd while some businesses closed early.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Volunteer snow observers reported that a total of 10 to 12 inches of snow fell across Olmsted County. An initial band of snow on the 21st provided 2 to 4 inches with an additional 7 to 9 inches falling late on the 22nd into the 23rd. The highest reported total was 13.5 inches near Rochester.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Areas of severe (D2) and extreme (D3) drought in southeast Minnesota along the Iowa border remained unchanged through the month of November. While October monthly rainfall totals were near or slightly above normal, the long-standing abnormally dry conditions comprised of total rainfall of 15 to 20 inches from April 1 to October 31st was 9 to 12 inches below normal. During November, only 1 to 3 days of measurable precipitation provided under 0.25 inches of total rainfall, as much as 2 inches below the monthly normal. While the growing season was all but over, multiple days of above normal temperatures, 10 to 25 degrees above normal, inhibited the ground from freezing. The drought remained unchanged through November as southern Mower, Fillmore, and Houston counties experienced persistent severe drought (D2), with extreme drought (D3) conditions right along the Iowa border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) to extreme (D3) drought persisted across the southern half of Fillmore County through November. The combination of total monthly precipitation near 0.25 inches, near 2 inches below normal, and anomalously warm temperatures, 10 to 20 degrees above normal for several days according to automated weather observation stations resulted in 30 to 40% below normal topsoil moisture across Fillmore County.

Heaviest snowfall occurred in southeast Minnesota during the late morning of Sunday, April 16th, where recorded snowfall totals were 12.0 near La Crescent (Houston County) and 10.5 near Pickwick (Winona County).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intermittent light snow from Saturday November 25th through Sunday November 26th resulted in slippery road conditions for the busy Thanksgiving holiday travel weekend. Minnesota Department of Transportation and broadcast media reported many slide offs and minor accidents across southeast Minnesota. While snowfall rates remained under 0.1 inches per hour for much of the weekend, a scattered increase to 0.25 inches per hour Sunday evening resulted in a temporary decrease in visibilities.  The longer duration resulted in a widespread 0.5 to 1.5 inches of overall accumulations by Sunday night. Highest snowfall reports were 2.0 inches near Stewartville (Olmsted County), and 1.5 inches near Caledonia (Houston County) and in Minnesota City (Winona County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A volunteer observer reported 0.8 inches of snowfall in Hayfield.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills around 35 below zero occurred across Olmsted County during the early morning of February 2nd. The coldest wind chill of 38 below occurred at a mesonet station south of Eyota.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band of heavy precipitation developed on the northern side of a surface low that tracked from southern Minnesota into Wisconsin. This band of precipitation started as rain on the morning of March 16, but transitioned over to snow during the day as it moved northeast. At the start of the event, temperatures were above freezing with wet roads, but they quickly become slush/snow covered as snowfall rates increased and northwest winds brought colder temperatures across the region. ||Several sites reported 1/4 mile visibility under the heavy snow band that produced a narrow swatch of 2 to 5 inches from southwest Minnesota through northeast Minnesota. A few totals were over 6 inches near St Cloud Minnesota, and to the northeast. After the snow ended, northwest winds continue during the afternoon which led to blizzard conditions across western Minnesota. The blizzard conditions were confined to areas that had the heavy snow earlier in the day, so this wasn't a true ground blizzard, but also wasn't limited to the time snow was falling. This was more of a hybrid of the two, where the deep snow pack became glazed over, and the new snow continually blew on top if it which reduced visibility and led to drifted snow on roadways. Below are a few of the notable reports.||Blizzard: |There were 4 to 5 foot drifts in both Chippewa and Renville county which made roads impassible. Lac Qui Parle closed state highways including 212, which is rare for them. MNDOT declared travel not advised across many roads across several counties in western/southwest Minnesota. Several dispatchers reported visibility near zero during the height of the event, with dozens of cars in the ditch. There were a few rescues, but overall the tow trucks were running so people just waited in their car to get towed out. No shelters were open for this event.||Snowfall: |6.1 inches near Rice Minnesota.|6.5 inches near Onamia Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A band of rain changed over to snow, with visibility dropping to 1/4 mile. Northwest winds increased as the snow was ending, and temperatures fell below freezing. This led to icy roads with significant blowing snow. Several roads were closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rapidly strengthening storm developed across the Northern Plains and into the western Great Lakes on March 16-17. While precipitation started in many locations as light rain or brief light freezing rain, a sharp fall in temperatures in the morning hours forced a change to a couple hours of heavy snowfall, accompanied by increasing northerly wind. With most ditches already full and snow depths the morning of March 16 between 7 and 20 inches, the new snowfall and winds which gusted as high as 45 to 55 mph led to the rapid development of significant snow drifts and widespread blowing snow with blizzard or near-blizzard conditions, on top of the added hazard of a flash freeze.||Numerous vehicles were stranded during the 16th and into early hours on the 17th in Cottonwood, Lyon and Murray counties. Seven motorists were forced to remain in vehicles overnight in Murray County, and over 60 travelers were put up in shelters in parts of Murray and Cottonwood Counties.||Snowfall across southwest Minnesota was generally from 2 to 4 inches, with only a light glaze of ice accumulation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Brief mixed precipitation quickly changed to a brief period of heavier snowfall which totaled 2.5 inches at the CoCoRAHS site 8 miles southeast of Hendricks. Ditches remained full from prior seasonal snow, and as northerly wind increased and gusted by mid-afternoon as high as 47 mph at the Minnesota Road Weather Information System sites MN030 near Hendricks and MN028 near Lake Benton, significant drifts to several feet and impassable roads were reported across much of the county. Visibility in open areas was frequently less than a quarter mile, often near zero at road level. MNDOT quickly placed the area in a no travel advised status with all state highways closed, which lasted into the following morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band of heavy precipitation developed on the northern side of a surface low that tracked from southern Minnesota into Wisconsin. This band of precipitation started as rain on the morning of March 16, but transitioned over to snow during the day as it moved northeast. At the start of the event, temperatures were above freezing with wet roads, but they quickly become slush/snow covered as snowfall rates increased and northwest winds brought colder temperatures across the region. ||Several sites reported 1/4 mile visibility under the heavy snow band that produced a narrow swatch of 2 to 5 inches from southwest Minnesota through northeast Minnesota. A few totals were over 6 inches near St Cloud Minnesota, and to the northeast. After the snow ended, northwest winds continue during the afternoon which led to blizzard conditions across western Minnesota. The blizzard conditions were confined to areas that had the heavy snow earlier in the day, so this wasn't a true ground blizzard, but also wasn't limited to the time snow was falling. This was more of a hybrid of the two, where the deep snow pack became glazed over, and the new snow continually blew on top if it which reduced visibility and led to drifted snow on roadways. Below are a few of the notable reports.||Blizzard: |There were 4 to 5 foot drifts in both Chippewa and Renville county which made roads impassible. Lac Qui Parle closed state highways including 212, which is rare for them. MNDOT declared travel not advised across many roads across several counties in western/southwest Minnesota. Several dispatchers reported visibility near zero during the height of the event, with dozens of cars in the ditch. There were a few rescues, but overall the tow trucks were running so people just waited in their car to get towed out. No shelters were open for this event.||Snowfall: |6.1 inches near Rice Minnesota.|6.5 inches near Onamia Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A band of rain changed over to snow, with visibility dropping to 1/4 mile. Most locations saw 3 to 5 inches of snow, with a few reports of just over 6 inches. Areas just east of Rice  received 6.1 inches. Northwest winds increased as the snow was ending, and temperatures fell below freezing. This led to icy roads with significant blowing snow. The combination of snow and strong winds created winter storm conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough brought widespread snow across the region. Most locations generally saw 3 to 5 inches, but a few locations picked up over 6 inches in a 24 hour period. Impacts were minimal since much of the snow fell during the day and the treated roads remained wet. This was not a winter storm warning event, but we are including it in storm data since we did have a few counties in a Winter Storm Warning. ||A few noteworthy snow amounts are:|3.5 inches near Alber Lea in Freeborn County.|3.2 inches near Ellendale in Steele County.|4.0 inches near Edina in Hennepin County.|4.0 inches near St Michael in Wright County.|3.8 inches north of Hutchinson in McLeod County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: No official snow measurements in this county, but there were two reports of 6.0 and 6.5 inches over a 24 hour period just across the border in eastern South Dakota. A Winter Storm Warning was in effect, but it did not verify.

A powerful late spring system produced a band of very heavy snow across the region on the evening of March 31, and ending during the early morning of April 01 (see corresponding entry for April). Snowfall rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour combined with northwest winds of 30 to near 40 mph hour. This was a heavy, wet snow, and as a result there were widespread power outages with the highest snowfall amounts across eastern Minnesota and western Wisconsin. In some areas, recovery lasted multiple days before full power was restored.||A report from the fire chief in Stillwater Minnesota (Washington County) said this was a major event, stating they had whiteout conditions from 0100 to 0230 AM. The amount of trees down and power outages were significant. I have not experienced this during a winter storm during my career.||A few notable snowfall amounts are:|13.0 inches near Oakdale in Washington County.|12.0 inches near Woodbury in Washington County.|12.0 inches near Monticello in Wright County.|12.0 inches near Medina in Hennepin County.|11.1 inches near Coon Rapids in Anoka County.|9.5 inches near Watertown in Carver County.|9.0 inches near North St Paul in Ramsey County.|9.0 inches near Eden Valley in Meeker County.|9.0 inches near Northfield in Dakota County.|8.5 inches near Prior Lake in Scott County.|8.4 inches near Winsted in McLeod County.|8.0 inches near Dundas in Rice County.|7.8 inches near Paynesville in Stearns County. |7.8 inches near New Prague in Le Sueur County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wintry mix with a couple hours of very heavy snow and strong northwest winds produced hazardous driving conditions. Based on surrounding reports, radar, ASOS observations, and MNDOT road conditions, this event verified at 0100 CST on April 01, before the snow ended during the early morning hours of April 01. A Co-op observer measured 4.7 inches total just across the eastern county line in Belview MN. In addition to the heavy snow, gusty winds near 40 mph led to periods of whiteout conditions and snow covered roads, which verified the winter storm.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds increased again during the later morning and afternoon of March 17 with frequent convective snow showers that briefly reduced visibility at times to one-half mile or less.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex storm system that brought strong to severe thunderstorms to parts of southeastern Minnesota during the late afternoon of Friday, March 31, 2023 brought a band of heavy snowfall from late in the evening into the early morning hours of April 1, 2023. Snowfall amounts of 5.0 inches were reported in Racine (Mower County), near Lansing (Mower County), and Mantorville (Dodge County). The highest total snowfall was 5.6 inches reported near Kasson (Dodge County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Volunteer snow observers reported that 5.0 inches of snow fell in parts of Mower County the evening of March 31st, 2023 into the early morning of April 1st, 2023. The highest reports were 5.0 inches in Racine and near Lansing.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rapidly strengthening storm developed across the Northern Plains and into the western Great Lakes on March 16-17.  An initial band of precipitation developed during the early morning hours of March 16 from eastern South Dakota into southwestern Minnesota. While precipitation started in many locations as light rain or brief light freezing rain, a sharp fall in temperatures in the morning hours forced a change to a couple hours of heavy snowfall, accompanied by increasing northerly wind. With most ditches already full and snow depths the morning of March 16 between 7 and 20 inches, the new snowfall and winds which gusted as high as 45 to 55 mph led to the rapid development of significant snow drifts and widespread blowing snow with blizzard or near-blizzard conditions, on top of the added hazard of a flash freeze.||Numerous vehicles were stranded during the 16th and into early hours on the 17th in Cottonwood, Lyon and Murray counties. Seven motorists were forced to remain in vehicles overnight in Murray County, and over 60 travelers were put up in shelters in parts of Murray and Cottonwood Counties.||Snowfall across southwest Minnesota was generally from 2 to 4 inches, with only a light glaze of ice accumulation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds increased again during the later morning and afternoon of March 17 with frequent convective snow showers that briefly reduced visibility at times to one-half mile or less.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system developed east of the Rockies in Colorado and then moved through the Great Plains and into the Upper Mississippi Valley from the evening of March 30th through the evening of March 31st. This system produced heavy snow across west central Minnesota, along with wind gusts of 35 to 45 mph. Some of the heaviest amounts include 9 inches a mile north-northeast of Browns Valley and 8 inches 2 miles northwest of Big Stone City. Significant blowing and drifting snow resulted. ||Impacts included numerous school closures, impassable or closed roads, many stranded vehicles and vehicle accidents, and power outages. In addition, this heavy snow further set the stage for significant spring flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A COOP observer measured 8 inches of snow 2 miles northwest of Big Stone City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band of heavy precipitation developed on the northern side of a surface low that tracked from southern Minnesota into Wisconsin. This band of precipitation started as rain on the morning of March 16, but transitioned over to snow during the day as it moved northeast. At the start of the event, temperatures were above freezing with wet roads, but they quickly become slush/snow covered as snowfall rates increased and northwest winds brought colder temperatures across the region. ||Several sites reported 1/4 mile visibility under the heavy snow band that produced a narrow swatch of 2 to 5 inches from southwest Minnesota through northeast Minnesota. A few totals were over 6 inches near St Cloud Minnesota, and to the northeast. After the snow ended, northwest winds continue during the afternoon which led to blizzard conditions across western Minnesota. The blizzard conditions were confined to areas that had the heavy snow earlier in the day, so this wasn't a true ground blizzard, but also wasn't limited to the time snow was falling. This was more of a hybrid of the two, where the deep snow pack became glazed over, and the new snow continually blew on top if it which reduced visibility and led to drifted snow on roadways. Below are a few of the notable reports.||Blizzard: |There were 4 to 5 foot drifts in both Chippewa and Renville county which made roads impassible. Lac Qui Parle closed state highways including 212, which is rare for them. MNDOT declared travel not advised across many roads across several counties in western/southwest Minnesota. Several dispatchers reported visibility near zero during the height of the event, with dozens of cars in the ditch. There were a few rescues, but overall the tow trucks were running so people just waited in their car to get towed out. No shelters were open for this event.||Snowfall: |6.1 inches near Rice Minnesota.|6.5 inches near Onamia Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation started as rain and changed over to snow, with 1 to 3 inches of accumulation. Northwest winds increased as well with falling temperatures which led to blowing snow and icy roads.

This was not a winter storm warning event, but we are including it in storm data since we did have a few counties in a Winter Storm Warning. ||A few noteworthy snow amounts are:|3.5 inches near Alber Lea in Freeborn County.|3.2 inches near Ellendale in Steele County.|4.0 inches near Edina in Hennepin County.|4.0 inches near St Michael in Wright County.|3.8 inches north of Hutchinson in McLeod County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure intensified and moved from the Central Plains into Minnesota. Initially, warm thermal profiles allowed for mixed precipitation or all rain. However, as winds shifted to the northwest, colder air rapidly changed precipitation over to snow. While additional accumulation of snow was fairly light (up to 1 to 3 inches in areas of greatest snowfall along an axis from Tyndall to Salem to Minneota), northwest winds were bolstered to a gusty 40 to 60 mph and resulted in widespread blowing snow along with spotty blizzard conditions near the Buffalo Ridge. Travel was not recommended during the overnight and early morning hours across parts of southwest Minnesota and east central South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several hours of gusty northwest winds reached a peak around 50 mph at Minnesota Road Weather Information System sites near Hendricks and Lake Benton. Despite snowfall only up to as high as 2.5 inches 8 miles southeast of Hendricks, winds whipped up widespread blowing snow with less than one-quarter mile visibility through several overnight and early morning hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure tracked from southwestern Minnesota into far northwestern Wisconsin on March 16th and generated between one and two inches of snow. Strong high pressure that followed resulted in a tight pressure gradient and strong northwest wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph that persisted between March 16th and 17th. Visibility was reduced from the blowing and drifting of both fresh and loose snow from the pre-existing deep snowpack, and hazardous travel was compounded when snow melted and then re-froze on road surfaces.    ||Hazardous or impassable roads due to low visibility and significant drifting snow resulted in numerous vehicle accidents and/or stranded motorists across the area.

While precipitation started in many locations as light rain or brief light freezing rain, a sharp fall in temperatures in the morning hours forced a change to a couple hours of heavy snowfall, accompanied by increasing northerly wind. With most ditches already full and snow depths the morning of March 16 between 7 and 20 inches, the new snowfall and winds which gusted as high as 45 to 55 mph led to the rapid development of significant snow drifts and widespread blowing snow with blizzard or near-blizzard conditions, on top of the added hazard of a flash freeze.||Numerous vehicles were stranded during the 16th and into early hours on the 17th in Cottonwood, Lyon and Murray counties. Seven motorists were forced to remain in vehicles overnight in Murray County, and over 60 travelers were put up in shelters in parts of Murray and Cottonwood Counties.||Snowfall across southwest Minnesota was generally from 2 to 4 inches, with only a light glaze of ice accumulation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Brief mixed precipitation quickly changed to a brief period of heavier snowfall which totaled 4.0 inches at the COOP site in Tracy and 3.0 inches at Minneota. Ditches remained full from prior seasonal snow, and as northerly wind increased and gusted by mid-afternoon as high as 58 mph at the Minnesota Road Weather Information System sites MN160 at Florence and MN156 near Taunton, and 46 mph at Automated Weather Observing System sites KMML at Marshall and KTKC at Tracy, significant drifts to several feet and impassable roads were reported across much of the county. Visibility in open areas was frequently less than a quarter mile, often near zero at road level. MNDOT quickly placed the area in a no travel advised status with all state highways closed, which lasted into the following morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light drizzle and freezing drizzle during the afternoon and evening of March 8th gradually mixed in with and transitioned to a light, long-duration snowfall through the day on March 9th in response to increasing 850 hPa warm air advection and low-level saturation. Snow slowly dissipated from west to east late in the evening on March 9th and into the early overnight on March 10th. Ice accumulations were a glaze at best given warmer ground temperatures and total snowfall ranged from 1 to 5 inches across the region. Winds were light through the event, resulting in minimal overall impact.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light drizzle or freezing drizzle slowly changed over to light snow, accumulating to 2.5 inches at the COOP site 2 miles northeast of Lakefield. Ice accumulation was minimal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rapidly strengthening storm developed across the Northern Plains and into the western Great Lakes on March 16-17.  An initial band of precipitation developed during the early morning hours of March 16 from eastern South Dakota into southwestern Minnesota. With most ditches already full and snow depths the morning of March 16 between 7 and 20 inches, the new snowfall and winds which gusted as high as 45 to 55 mph led to the rapid development of significant snow drifts and widespread blowing snow with blizzard or near-blizzard conditions, on top of the added hazard of a flash freeze.||Numerous vehicles were stranded during the 16th and into early hours on the 17th in Cottonwood, Lyon and Murray counties. Seven motorists were forced to remain in vehicles overnight in Murray County, and over 60 travelers were put up in shelters in parts of Murray and Cottonwood Counties.||Snowfall across southwest Minnesota was generally from 2 to 4 inches, with only a light glaze of ice accumulation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After a brief period of rain and freezing rain with less than a tenth of an inch ice accumulation, precipitation changed to a brief period of heavier snowfall which brought 1.5 inches to the COOP observer site in Hills. As winds increased on March 16 with gusts up to 51 mph at Minnesota Road Weather Information System Sites MN006 in Beaver Creek and MN147 near Magnolia, spotty locations saw visibility briefly down to one-half mile or less at times during the late morning and afternoon. MNDOT quickly placed the area in a no travel advised status with all state highways and Interstate 90 closed, which lasted into the following morning. Winds increased again during the later morning and afternoon of March 17 with frequent convective snow showers that briefly reduced visibility at times to one-half mile or less.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure intensified and moved from the Central Plains into Minnesota. Initially, warm thermal profiles allowed for mixed precipitation or all rain. However, as winds shifted to the northwest, colder air rapidly changed precipitation over to snow. While additional accumulation of snow was fairly light (up to 1 to 3 inches in areas of greatest snowfall along an axis from Tyndall to Salem to Minneota), northwest winds were bolstered to a gusty 40 to 60 mph and resulted in widespread blowing snow along with spotty blizzard conditions near the Buffalo Ridge. Travel was not recommended during the overnight and early morning hours across parts of southwest Minnesota and east central South Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Northwest wind reached gusts to 41 mph at the RAWS site at Pipestone. Despite only an inch of new snowfall at Pipestone, winds whipped up considerable blowing snow with visibility of a mile or less at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon of May 06 a weak frontal boundary was draped across western Minnesota. Afternoon clearing allowed a few hundred Joules of MLCAPE to develop, and storm relative helicity increased along and north of the boundary. A few rotating thunderstorms developed and produced several brief, weak tornadoes, along with one report of quarter size hail. This was a low end severe weather event, with little impacts.||The only damage report was to an out building. Video shows part of the roof being blown off.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 1831 CST storm chaser video confirmed a weak tornado developed near 260th St in Renville County. This storm continued into Kandiyohi county towards the city of Prinsburg, MN. No injuries or damages were reported.

Afternoon clearing allowed a few hundred Joules of MLCAPE to develop, and storm relative helicity increased along and north of the boundary. This was a low end severe weather event, with little impacts.||The only damage report was to an out building. Video shows part of the roof being blown off.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This segment is a continuation of the tornado that initially spun up in the Renville County and tracked into southern Kandiyohi County. At approximately 1833 CST this storm crossed the southern border of Kandiyohi County towards the town of Prinsburg, MN before lifting at 1836CST. AT 1834CST the local fire department reported this tornado in an open field southeast of Prinsburg. There were no reports of damage or fatalities with this storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band of heavy precipitation developed on the northern side of a surface low that tracked from southern Minnesota into Wisconsin. This band of precipitation started as rain on the morning of March 16, but transitioned over to snow during the day as it moved northeast. At the start of the event, temperatures were above freezing with wet roads, but they quickly become slush/snow covered as snowfall rates increased and northwest winds brought colder temperatures across the region. ||Several sites reported 1/4 mile visibility under the heavy snow band that produced a narrow swatch of 2 to 5 inches from southwest Minnesota through northeast Minnesota. A few totals were over 6 inches near St Cloud Minnesota, and to the northeast. After the snow ended, northwest winds continue during the afternoon which led to blizzard conditions across western Minnesota. The blizzard conditions were confined to areas that had the heavy snow earlier in the day, so this wasn't a true ground blizzard, but also wasn't limited to the time snow was falling. This was more of a hybrid of the two, where the deep snow pack became glazed over, and the new snow continually blew on top if it which reduced visibility and led to drifted snow on roadways. Below are a few of the notable reports.||Blizzard: |There were 4 to 5 foot drifts in both Chippewa and Renville county which made roads impassible. Lac Qui Parle closed state highways including 212, which is rare for them. MNDOT declared travel not advised across many roads across several counties in western/southwest Minnesota. Several dispatchers reported visibility near zero during the height of the event, with dozens of cars in the ditch. There were a few rescues, but overall the tow trucks were running so people just waited in their car to get towed out. No shelters were open for this event.||Snowfall: |6.1 inches near Rice Minnesota.|6.5 inches near Onamia Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A band of rain changed over to snow, with visibility dropping to 1/4 mile. Northwest winds increased as the snow was ending, and temperatures fell below freezing. This led to icy roads with significant blowing snow. Several roads were closed in the county, including Hwy 212.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure moved from the central Plains early morning on March 31 into the western Great Lakes by early morning April 1. The area was just on the south side of the more significant banded precipitation, and featured enough instability for a little thunder to mix in during the morning hours. Most precipitation started as rain or freezing rain, mixing with and changing to snow through the afternoon and early evening from west to east. Winds behind the system increased to a gusty 30 to 40 mph at the peak, with snowfall generally from 2 to 4 inches north of I-90 and west of the James River, as well as southwest Minnesota mainly north of Highway 14. The combination of wind and snowfall led to some near-blizzard conditions for a brief time during the late evening and early overnight hours of April 1.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light freezing rain and mixed precipitation produced a glaze of icing, before precipitation changed to snow during the evening. Total snow accumulation of 1.5 inches was reported at a CoCoRaHS site 8 miles southeast of Hendricks. Northerly winds reached gusts as high as 43 mph at Minnesota Road Weather Information System site MN028 near Lake Benton, with visibilities briefly one mile or less between 2100-2400CST.

Total snow accumulation of 3.0 inches was reported at COOP site MNNM5 at Minneota, 2.5 inches at COOP site MMLM5 at Marshall, and 3.5 inches at a CoCoRaHS site 5 miles south of Cottonwood. Northerly winds reached gusts as high as 46 mph at Minnesota Road Weather Information System site MN156 near Taunton and 40 mph at Automated Weather Observation System site KMML at Marshall, with visibilities one mile or less between 2200CST March 31 and 0200CST April 1.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful late spring system produced a band of very heavy snow across the region on the evening of March 31, and ending during the early morning of April 01. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure moved from southeastern Colorado into the Upper Mississippi Valley April 4th into April 5th and brought a wintry mix of rain, snow and freezing rain to much of the Northland. Freezing rain was most impactful with this system, especially across areas of northwest Wisconsin adjacent to Lake Superior where up to 0.30 inches of ice accumulated. Northeastern Minnesota saw more of a mix of snow and freezing rain with the Brainerd Lakes area receiving upwards of a quarter of an inch of ice along with a few inches of snow. Areas along the North Shore of Lake Superior received more in the way of snow with just over 6 inches received in areas along the terrain ridge. Strong wind gusts over 60 mph were recorded along the North Shore as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals of 3 to 5 inches were recorded across the southern portion of the county with a high report of 6.2 in Silver Bay.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure moved from southeastern Colorado into the Upper Mississippi Valley April 4th into April 5th and brought a wintry mix of rain, snow and freezing rain to much of the Northland. Freezing rain was most impactful with this system, especially across areas of northwest Wisconsin adjacent to Lake Superior where up to 0.30 inches of ice accumulated. Northeastern Minnesota saw more of a mix of snow and freezing rain with the Brainerd Lakes area receiving upwards of a quarter of an inch of ice along with a few inches of snow. Areas along the North Shore of Lake Superior received more in the way of snow with just over 6 inches received in areas along the terrain ridge. Strong wind gusts over 60 mph were recorded along the North Shore as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals of 2 to 4 inches were common across the southern part of the county with a high report of 6.1 in Hovland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Mustinka River and its tributaries experienced flooding due to the rapid snowmelt in western Minnesota caused by frozen soils and a quick thaw in the second week of April. The area, including Wheaton, saw a significant snow depth of 32 inches on April 6th. As a result, a large amount of snowmelt flowed into the waterways, leading to numerous county and township roads being submerged. To safeguard properties, many communities initiated sandbagging operations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The West Branch of 12 Mile Creek at Dumont went into flood on the evening of April 13th, cresting 1.97 feet above flood stage on the evening of April 15th. Agricultural lands were impacted. Flood waters inundated the city of Dumont sewer system causing water to back up into streets and basements.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Gravity waves due to ducting generated a narrow line of strong wind across Central Minnesota to Eastern Minnesota on the morning of April 19th, 2023. Early that morning a weak system produced precipitation across the region. Upper-Air soundings from NWS Twin Cities detailed a low-level inversion/stable layer at approximately 6,000 feet above the surface with remnant instability from the previous rainfall early that morning. Over the Central Plains, low pressure was developing an associated warm front that extended over Iowa was advancing towards Minnesota. Meanwhile in the upper-levels, a trough was beginning to dig over the Western Dakotas. As these features progress northeastward, remnant low-level forcing would interact with the capped layer and be refracted back towards the ground and creating a line of gusty winds across the surface. ||A few isolated reports of strong winds occurred across the northern half of the Twin Cities metro area. In Wright County, Buffalo, MN ASOS station recorded at 48 mph gust just before 9 AM CST. A section of wooden fence was pushed over in a neighborhood located in St. Michael, MN. Farther east in Ramsey County, a few small trees were downed as a result of strong winds in Maplewood, MN.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong easterly winds were recorded near St. Michael, MN area where parts of a fence were blown over. Buffalo, MN AWOS station recorded a 48 mph wind gust at 8:55 am.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving, deepening area of low pressure located over the Upper Peninsula of Michigan affected the Northland on the 16th into the 17th bringing a period of freezing rain before changing over to snow. Areas closer to the low in northwest Wisconsin received the brunt of the snow, but pockets of heavier snowfall to 6 to 8 inches were reported downwind of Lake Superior due to lake enhancement. As the low deepened the night of the 16th into the 17th, gusty winds led to a period of blizzard conditions along the North Shore of Lake Superior during the morning of the 17th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall varied greatly across the northern reaches of Lake and Cook counties with little to no snow in northern areas to around 8-10 inches in the far southern areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful late spring system produced a band of very heavy snow across the region on the evening of March 31, and ending during the early morning of April 01. Snowfall rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour combined with northwest winds of 30 to near 40 mph hour. This was a heavy, wet snow, and as a result there were widespread power outages with the highest snowfall amounts across eastern Minnesota and western Wisconsin. In some areas, recovery lasted multiple days before full power was restored.||A report from the fire chief in Stillwater Minnesota (Washington County) said this was a major event. They had whiteout conditions from 0100 to 0230 AM. The amount of trees down and power outages were significant. I have not experienced this during a winter storm during my career.||A few noteable snowfall amounts are:|13.0 inches near Oakdale in Washington County.|12.0 inches near Woodbury in Washington County.|12.0 inches near Monticello in Wright County.|12.0 inches near Medina in Hennepin County.|11.1 inches near Coon Rapids in Anoka County.|9.5 inches near Watertown in Carver County.|9.0 inches near North St Paul in Ramsey County.|9.0 inches near Eden Valley in Meeker County.|9.0 inches near Northfield in Dakota County.|8.5 inches near Prior Lake in Scott County.|8.4 inches near Winsted in McLeod County.|8.0 inches near Dundas in Rice County.|7.8 inches near Paynesville in Stearns County. |7.8 inches near New Prague in Le Sueur County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This is a continuation of the winter storm that began on March 31. A wintry mix with a couple hours of very heavy snow and strong northwest winds produced hazardous driving conditions.  Based on surrounding reports, radar, ASOS observations, and MNDOT road conditions, this event verified at 0100 CST on April 01, before the snow ended during the early morning hours of April 01. A Co-op observer measured 5.5 inches near Norway Lake, and 8.5 inches in the northeast part of the county near Regal. In addition to the heavy snow, gusty winds near 40 mph led to periods of whiteout conditions and snow covered roads, which verified the winter storm.
