EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A near-record snowfall season across Minnesota and Wisconsin led to a snowpack with snow-water-equivilent (SWE) values of 4 to 6 inches across much of the region by mid-March. Below normal temperatures for the first of of spring kept much of the snowpack in tact until the end of the month when it started to melt.||The melt accelerated in early April. On April 08, highs were in the 60s across the region. On April 09-10 they were in the 70s, and during April 11-14, many locations saw highs in the 80s! This rapid snowmelt led to widespread flooding across the low-lying areas, ditches and ravines. As a result, many gravel roads and culverts were washed out or needed to be repaired, costing nearly $2.3 million in damages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A combination of record setting seasonal snowfall, high snow water equivalent values, and rapid snow melt led to low lying areas, ditches, and ravines across the county to flood for a prolonged period of time. Road infrastructure such as bridges, gravel roads, and culverts experience varied degrees of damage caused by rapid runoff. Stevens County Emergency Management reported $145,700 in damages to roads and bridges.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving, deepening area of low pressure located over the Upper Peninsula of Michigan affected the Northland on the 16th into the 17th bringing a period of freezing rain before changing over to snow. Areas closer to the low in northwest Wisconsin received the brunt of the snow, but pockets of heavier snowfall to 6 to 8 inches were reported downwind of Lake Superior due to lake enhancement. As the low deepened the night of the 16th into the 17th, gusty winds led to a period of blizzard conditions along the North Shore of Lake Superior during the morning of the 17th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals around the southern part of the county were in the 1 to 4 inch range but some enhancement was observed near the Iron Range with 6.5 inches reported in Keewatin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On April 12th, the Minnesota River at Ortonville surpassed flood stage, reaching a peak crest of 12.03 feet on April 16th. To maintain the flow between Big Stone Lake, the Whetstone River, and the Minnesota River, a backhoe was utilized at the flood barriers to prevent debris and ice from causing disruptions. The rising floodwaters posed a significant threat to numerous lakeside properties along Big Stone Lake, necessitating residents to engage in sandbagging efforts. Additionally, various agricultural, forested, and park lands were submerged due to the extensive flooding.

The Mustinka River and its tributaries experienced flooding due to the rapid snowmelt in western Minnesota caused by frozen soils and a quick thaw in the second week of April. The area, including Wheaton, saw a significant snow depth of 32 inches on April 6th. As a result, a large amount of snowmelt flowed into the waterways, leading to numerous county and township roads being submerged. To safeguard properties, many communities initiated sandbagging operations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Twelve Mile Creek above Wheaton crested above moderate flood stage at 19.4 feet on the afternoon of April 15th. Waters were high enough to flood portions of 670th Street, however the main impact was flooding of agricultural lands.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple bands of accumulating snow. ||A long duration winter storm produced multiple bands of widespread significant snowfall across Minnesota, with persistent northwest winds developing as the storm exited the region. Snow began to fall on the morning of February 21st and persisted in waves through the morning of February 23rd. Wind gust observations were in the low to mid 30 mph range. Overall snow accumulations provided by a combination of trained spotters and the CoCoRaHS observers ranged from 8-16 inches across the region, with a few localized higher amounts. ||Some of the highest snowfall totals across the area include:||16.8 inches near Victoria in Carver County.|16.6 inches near Prior Lake in Scott County.|16.5 inches near Danube in Renville County.|16.5 inches near Faribault in Rice County.|16.1 inches near New Prague in Le Sueur County.|16.0 inches near Prinsburg in Kandiyohi County.|15.9 inches near Cambridge in Isanti County.|15.7 inches near Waseca in Waseca County.|15.4 inches near Stark in Chisago County.|15.3 inches near Corvuso in Meeker County.|15.1 inches near Luxemburg in Stearns County.|15.0 inches near Little Canada in Ramsey County.|15.0 inches near South St Paul in Dakota County.|14.6 inches near New Ulm in Brown County.|14.5 inches near Bethel in Anoka County.|14.2 inches near Edina in Hennepin County.|14.0 inches near Mora in Mille Lacs County.|12.2 inches near Morris in Stevens County.|11.3 inches near Albert Lea in Freeborn County.|11.6 inches near Winnebago in Faribault County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple bands of snow moved across the county. Sustained winds increased out of the northeast near 15-20 mph with gusts 25-30 mph per the Mankato Airport ASOS observations. Across the county, Interpolated Snowfall Analysis using the National Gridded Snowfall Analysis (NGSA) map detailed that at least 3-6 inches fell in 24 hrs, 4-12 inches in 48 hrs making an overall total range of 10-18 inches. CoCoRaHS and Trained Spotter observations helped verify the NGSA by showing highest snowfall observations evenly distributed across the county with 12-14 measured inches. The highest reported observation was 14.3 inches in Henderson, MN or 5 miles north of Le Sueur, MN.||Snowfall combined with gusty winds led to difficult travel conditions which ultimately led to the Department of Transportation and MN State Patrol advising against travel for much of Western MN.

To maintain the flow between Big Stone Lake, the Whetstone River, and the Minnesota River, a backhoe was utilized at the flood barriers to prevent debris and ice from causing disruptions. EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Minnesota River at Big Stone Lake reached a level of 972.7 or 1.2 feet above flood. An unknown number of cabins and businesses along the lake experienced some degree of flooding or water damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During early April, an unprecedented snow depth of 15 to 20 inches was recorded. However, a few weeks later, warmer temperatures triggered a rapid snowmelt that occurred over a span of four to five days in mid-April. This quick thaw led to extensive overland flooding in Traverse and Big Stone Counties, resulting in severe impacts to public and personal infrastructure. To protect their properties, residents took proactive measures by initiating sandbagging operations. Unfortunately, the floodwaters caused the sewer system in the city of Dumont to be overwhelmed, resulting in water backing up into streets and basements. Additionally, numerous county and township roads, as well as widespread agricultural lands, were inundated due to the snowmelt. The floodwaters gradually receded during the evening hours of April 17th.

A strong area of low pressure advanced across the Central United States. Given its track, the northern sector of this storm contained plentiful moisture and supply of warm air advection from the south. Mixed precipitation types were observed with this storm. For Eastern Minnesota, most areas would observe a mix of rain and snow in the overnight hours Sunday night which transitioned to freezing rain during the daytime hours Monday. A winter weather advisory was issued for freezing rain, occasionally mixed with snow. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During early April, an unprecedented snow depth of 15 to 20 inches was recorded. However, a few weeks later, warmer temperatures triggered a rapid snowmelt that occurred over a span of four to five days in mid-April. This quick thaw led to extensive overland flooding in Traverse and Big Stone Counties, resulting in severe impacts to public and personal infrastructure. To protect their properties, residents took proactive measures by initiating sandbagging operations. Unfortunately, the floodwaters caused the sewer system in the city of Dumont to be overwhelmed, resulting in water backing up into streets and basements. Additionally, numerous county and township roads, as well as widespread agricultural lands, were inundated due to the snowmelt. The floodwaters gradually receded during the evening hours of April 17th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large low-pressure system proceeded across Central and Southern Minnesota. Strong warm air advection provided via southerly flow allowed surface temperatures to create a multi-phased precipitation event. ||The environment was abnormally moist for the month of February which set a record precipitable water (0.56 inches) value. Initially, this event began in the form of rain during the daytime hours of Feb 14th. As solar heating decreased through the evening, the rainfall phased into a rain/snow mix and eventually all snow by the morning hours of the 15th. There was a centralized area of heaviest rainfall that occurred in Eastern Minnesota and the St. Croix River Valley. CoCoRaHS observations in areas such as Hennepin County near Plymouth, MN down to Chanhassen MN recorded near 1.2 to 1.3 total inches of rain. Areas in Anoka County such as East Bethel and Ham Lake observed between 1.25 and 1.3 inches of rainfall.  In Isanti County near Cambridge, 1.26 inches of rain. The highest observed amount was located in Washington County near Afton, MN with 1.34 inches of rain. ||The overall structure of the low-pressure was fairly stacked or in other words vertically aligned.  A troughing pattern in the upper-levels was combined with a surface low which in turn supported a tight pressure gradient that could generate strong wind gusts between 40-50+ mph.  Numerous airfield stations across Western MN captured strong winds gusts in the early morning hours of Feb 15th between 1 AM to 10 AM. Madison Airport in Lac Qui Parle County observed the strongest recorded gust at 58 mph. Morris AP (Stevens), Redwood Falls AP (Redwood), St. James (Watonwan), and Fairmont (Martin) observed 53 mph gusts. Lastly Benson AP (Swift) and Montevideo AP (Chippewa) observed just above 50 mph gusts. No wind damage was reported from any of these observations. ||As a result of increased wind speeds and precipitation phasing to all snow, blowing snow became a factor in Western Minnesota. Specifically, county law enforcement or emergency management in Stevens, Swift, Chippewa, Lac Qui Parle, Yellow Medicine, and Douglas counties all reported receiving numerous calls for motorists stuck in ditches. They also reported the visibility was very poor, there were sporadic snow drifts on roadways making travel impossible. ||For the extreme Western Minnesota in terms of snowfall accumulations, CoCoRaHS observations recorded 2.2 inches northwest of Morris, MN (Stevens). The remainder of nearby observations sites recorded amounts between 1-2 inches of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law Enforcement reported that there were no references to whiteout conditions however, multiple vehicles went off the road near Hwy 15 and Hwy 60 in the early morning. One incident included a vehicle rolling onto its side. No reported injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few storms developed during the afternoon along a cold frontal boundary with up to 2000 J/kg available CAPE and 40 to 50 knots of effective shear. Rotating storms produced large hail, and isolated very large hail across southwest Minnesota.

The Mustinka River and its tributaries experienced flooding due to the rapid snowmelt in western Minnesota caused by frozen soils and a quick thaw in the second week of April. The area, including Wheaton, saw a significant snow depth of 32 inches on April 6th. As a result, a large amount of snowmelt flowed into the waterways, leading to numerous county and township roads being submerged. To safeguard properties, many communities initiated sandbagging operations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mustinka River at Wheaton crested at 94.78 feet on the afternoon of April 17th, or 2.78 feet above flood stage. This high water mainly impacted agricultural lands.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Using Mn511.org traffic cameras and road condition reports, a few spin outs occurred along the I-94 corridor due to freezing drizzle that occurred in the early morning in between Brooklyn Park, MN and northwest towards Rogers, MN.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large low-pressure system proceeded across Central and Southern Minnesota. Strong warm air advection provided via southerly flow allowed surface temperatures to create a multi-phased precipitation event. ||The environment was abnormally moist for the month of February which set a record precipitable water (0.56 inches) value. Initially, this event began in the form of rain during the daytime hours of Feb 14th. As solar heating decreased through the evening, the rainfall phased into a rain/snow mix and eventually all snow by the morning hours of the 15th. There was a centralized area of heaviest rainfall that occurred in Eastern Minnesota and the St. Croix River Valley. CoCoRaHS observations in areas such as Hennepin County near Plymouth, MN down to Chanhassen MN recorded near 1.2 to 1.3 total inches of rain. Areas in Anoka County such as East Bethel and Ham Lake observed between 1.25 and 1.3 inches of rainfall.  In Isanti County near Cambridge, 1.26 inches of rain. The highest observed amount was located in Washington County near Afton, MN with 1.34 inches of rain. ||The overall structure of the low-pressure was fairly stacked or in other words vertically aligned.  A troughing pattern in the upper-levels was combined with a surface low which in turn supported a tight pressure gradient that could generate strong wind gusts between 40-50+ mph.  Numerous airfield stations across Western MN captured strong winds gusts in the early morning hours of Feb 15th between 1 AM to 10 AM. Madison Airport in Lac Qui Parle County observed the strongest recorded gust at 58 mph. Morris AP (Stevens), Redwood Falls AP (Redwood), St. James (Watonwan), and Fairmont (Martin) observed 53 mph gusts. Lastly Benson AP (Swift) and Montevideo AP (Chippewa) observed just above 50 mph gusts. No wind damage was reported from any of these observations. ||As a result of increased wind speeds and precipitation phasing to all snow, blowing snow became a factor in Western Minnesota. Specifically, county law enforcement or emergency management in Stevens, Swift, Chippewa, Lac Qui Parle, Yellow Medicine, and Douglas counties all reported receiving numerous calls for motorists stuck in ditches. They also reported the visibility was very poor, there were sporadic snow drifts on roadways making travel impossible. ||For the extreme Western Minnesota in terms of snowfall accumulations, CoCoRaHS observations recorded 2.2 inches northwest of Morris, MN (Stevens). The remainder of nearby observations sites recorded amounts between 1-2 inches of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County Sheriff reported winds and whiteout and zero visibility across the county for multiple hours between 4 AM to 10 AM. Numerous calls for assistance for stranded vehicles due to blowing/drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system developed across the western Great Plains and strengthened as it tracked northeast into Iowa on the afternoon of April 4th and into northeastern Minnesota by noon on April 5th. This system produced accumulating snow across west central Minnesota on April 4th, and then strong west-northwest wind gusts between 30 and 45 mph on April 5th. The result was significantly reduced visibilities at times. Ditches were full of snow from this and previous snowfall events, which allowed for significant drifts to develop on roadways as well.||Many US and state highways were either closed or under no travel advised statements from the Department of Transportation. Business and school closures resulted, and there were several reports of power outages. Following this storm, many locations had observed a top-10 winter season for total snowfall across the region. All the snow and much below average temperatures to start April set the stage for significant spring flooding.

Madison Airport in Lac Qui Parle County observed the strongest recorded gust at 58 mph. Morris AP (Stevens), Redwood Falls AP (Redwood), St. James (Watonwan), and Fairmont (Martin) observed 53 mph gusts. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system developed across the western Great Plains and strengthened as it tracked northeast into Iowa on the afternoon of April 4th and into northeastern Minnesota by noon on April 5th. This system produced accumulating snow across west central Minnesota on April 4th, and then strong west-northwest wind gusts between 30 and 45 mph on April 5th. All the snow and much below average temperatures to start April set the stage for significant spring flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep area of low pressure east of the region and a strong high pressure building into the region from the north produced high winds across west central Minnesota. A peak wind gust of 58 mph was observed 3 miles west of Odessa.

A large low-pressure system proceeded across Central and Southern Minnesota. Strong warm air advection provided via southerly flow allowed surface temperatures to create a multi-phased precipitation event. ||The environment was abnormally moist for the month of February which set a record precipitable water (0.56 inches) value. Initially, this event began in the form of rain during the daytime hours of Feb 14th. As solar heating decreased through the evening, the rainfall phased into a rain/snow mix and eventually all snow by the morning hours of the 15th. There was a centralized area of heaviest rainfall that occurred in Eastern Minnesota and the St. Croix River Valley. CoCoRaHS observations in areas such as Hennepin County near Plymouth, MN down to Chanhassen MN recorded near 1.2 to 1.3 total inches of rain. Areas in Anoka County such as East Bethel and Ham Lake observed between 1.25 and 1.3 inches of rainfall.  In Isanti County near Cambridge, 1.26 inches of rain. The highest observed amount was located in Washington County near Afton, MN with 1.34 inches of rain. ||The overall structure of the low-pressure was fairly stacked or in other words vertically aligned.  A troughing pattern in the upper-levels was combined with a surface low which in turn supported a tight pressure gradient that could generate strong wind gusts between 40-50+ mph.  Numerous airfield stations across Western MN captured strong winds gusts in the early morning hours of Feb 15th between 1 AM to 10 AM. Madison Airport in Lac Qui Parle County observed the strongest recorded gust at 58 mph. Morris AP (Stevens), Redwood Falls AP (Redwood), St. James (Watonwan), and Fairmont (Martin) observed 53 mph gusts. Lastly Benson AP (Swift) and Montevideo AP (Chippewa) observed just above 50 mph gusts. No wind damage was reported from any of these observations. ||As a result of increased wind speeds and precipitation phasing to all snow, blowing snow became a factor in Western Minnesota. Specifically, county law enforcement or emergency management in Stevens, Swift, Chippewa, Lac Qui Parle, Yellow Medicine, and Douglas counties all reported receiving numerous calls for motorists stuck in ditches. They also reported the visibility was very poor, there were sporadic snow drifts on roadways making travel impossible. ||For the extreme Western Minnesota in terms of snowfall accumulations, CoCoRaHS observations recorded 2.2 inches northwest of Morris, MN (Stevens). The remainder of nearby observations sites recorded amounts between 1-2 inches of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County Emergency Manager reported that multiple road incidents occurred due poor road conditions. Notably one incident included an accident with minor injuries in Spicer, MN where an armored truck collided with a light pole.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple bands of accumulating snow. ||A long duration winter storm produced multiple bands of widespread significant snowfall across Minnesota, with persistent northwest winds developing as the storm exited the region. Snow began to fall on the morning of February 21st and persisted in waves through the morning of February 23rd. Wind gust observations were in the low to mid 30 mph range. Overall snow accumulations provided by a combination of trained spotters and the CoCoRaHS observers ranged from 8-16 inches across the region, with a few localized higher amounts. ||Some of the highest snowfall totals across the area include:||16.8 inches near Victoria in Carver County.|16.6 inches near Prior Lake in Scott County.|16.5 inches near Danube in Renville County.|16.5 inches near Faribault in Rice County.|16.1 inches near New Prague in Le Sueur County.|16.0 inches near Prinsburg in Kandiyohi County.|15.9 inches near Cambridge in Isanti County.|15.7 inches near Waseca in Waseca County.|15.4 inches near Stark in Chisago County.|15.3 inches near Corvuso in Meeker County.|15.1 inches near Luxemburg in Stearns County.|15.0 inches near Little Canada in Ramsey County.|15.0 inches near South St Paul in Dakota County.|14.6 inches near New Ulm in Brown County.|14.5 inches near Bethel in Anoka County.|14.2 inches near Edina in Hennepin County.|14.0 inches near Mora in Mille Lacs County.|12.2 inches near Morris in Stevens County.|11.3 inches near Albert Lea in Freeborn County.|11.6 inches near Winnebago in Faribault County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple bands of snow moved across the county. Sustained winds increased out of the northeast near 15-20 mph with gusts near 25-30 mph per the Benson Municipal Airport ASOS observations. Across the county, Interpolated Snowfall Analysis using the National Gridded Snowfall Analysis (NGSA) map detailed that at least 1-3 inches fell in 24 hrs, 6-12 inches in 48 hrs making an overall total range of 7-15 inches. CoCoRaHS and Trained Spotter observations helped verify the NGSA by showing highest snowfall observations evenly distributed across the county with 11-13 measured inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few storms developed during the afternoon along a cold frontal boundary with up to 2000 J/kg available CAPE and 40 to 50 knots of effective shear. Rotating storms produced large hail, and isolated very large hail across southwest Minnesota.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm impacted the southern red river valley and MN Lakes country from February 28th through March 1st, 2023. While warnings were issued in the early morning hours of February 28th, snow did not begin until the afternoon and continued to intensify into March 1st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving area of low pressure brought a long duration snowfall to much of the Northland starting the 11th and continuing into the 12th. Areas near the track of the low received around a foot of snow and then some, especially with terrain influence along the North Shore where upwards of 18 inches of snow were reported. Snowfall was heavy and wet for this event and, when combined with the well above normal snowfall received already this season, a section of the roof at the Miller Hill Mall in Duluth collapsed a few days after the snow. Strong winds off Lake Superior led to blizzard conditions in the Duluth area on the 12th with winds gusting to 56 mph at Sky Harbor airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals of 3 to 7 inches were reported across the northern part of the county with a high report of 9.2 inches near Ely.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon of May 06 a weak frontal boundary was draped across western Minnesota. Afternoon clearing allowed a few hundred Joules of MLCAPE to develop, and storm relative helicity increased along and north of the boundary. A few rotating thunderstorms developed and produced several brief, weak tornadoes, along with one report of quarter size hail. This was a low end severe weather event, with little impacts.||The only damage report was to an out building. Video shows part of the roof being blown off.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter measured quarter size hail southeast of Prinsburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quick moving clipper system brought a swath of heavy snow to mainly the southern half of the Northland through the day on the 1st. Snowfall totals of 4 to 8 inches were common along and south of the Iron Range. A small area of higher amounts on the order of 12 to 14 inches was embedded withing this area of snowfall. Strong winds on the backside of the system led to blowing and drifting snow into the 2nd before high pressure built in.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts were generally in the 2-5 inch range across the central portion of St. Louis County, but a total of 6 inches was recorded near Island Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak area of low pressure tracked from west central South Dakota on the afternoon of February 28th to southwestern Minnesota by the late morning of March 1st. It produced periods of snow on the 28th and then a shield of snowfall along with more localized bands of heavier snows through the morning of March 1st. Snowfall totals ranged from 2 to 6 plus inches, the highest report being 6.5 inches in Wheaton.||In addition to the snow, strong wind gusts of up to 45 mph led to greatly reduced visibility at times. Slick and snow-covered roads also contributed to vehicle accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six and a half inches of snow was observed in Wheaton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few weak thunderstorms developed during the evening of the 8th due to daytime heating. Most of the storms remained well below severe limits, but one storm did produce nickel-sized hail in Carlton County during the early morning hours. The hail was not discovered until the next morning and was likely larger when it fell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A photo was received showing hail piles taken after sunrise. The largest stones were nickel-sized and were most likely larger when they fell some 4-5 hours earlier.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms developed ahead of a cold front across the region during the afternoon hours of the 30th. Conditions were marginal for severe weather, but a few storms in the Leech Lake area were able to produce hail of nickel to quarter size briefly before weakening.

A quick moving clipper system brought a swath of heavy snow to mainly the southern half of the Northland through the day on the 1st. Snowfall totals of 4 to 8 inches were common along and south of the Iron Range. A small area of higher amounts on the order of 12 to 14 inches was embedded withing this area of snowfall. Strong winds on the backside of the system led to blowing and drifting snow into the 2nd before high pressure built in.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals of 4 to 7 inches were reported across southern Itasca County with a high report of 7.8 inches in Jacobson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon of May 06 a weak frontal boundary was draped across western Minnesota. Afternoon clearing allowed a few hundred Joules of MLCAPE to develop, and storm relative helicity increased along and north of the boundary. A few rotating thunderstorms developed and produced several brief, weak tornadoes, along with one report of quarter size hail. This was a low end severe weather event, with little impacts.||The only damage report was to an out building. Video shows part of the roof being blown off.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 1818 CST storm chaser video confirmed a weak tornado developed southeast of 20699 860th Street. No injuries or damages were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system progressed through the Central Plains during the afternoon of May 6th, producing large hail across parts of Iowa as it lifted northeast. As the storms moved into southeast Minnesota that evening, they reinvigorated into a line and produced quarter to half dollar sized hail in parts of Wabasha County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Volunteer observers reported half dollar sized hail near Lake City during the evening of May 6th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of heavy rain and anomalously warm temperatures melting a deeper than average snowpack during April resulted in moderate to major flooding at sites along the Upper Mississippi River in Minnesota. Initial minor flood stage was reached in mid April with near record crests towards the end of April. Drier conditions during the latter half of April and well below normal rainfall through May allowed the Upper Mississippi River to steadily recede, dropping below minor flood stage at sites in Minnesota during the beginning of May.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The combination of heavy rainfall and rapidly melting snow resulted in the fifth highest crest on record at the Winona Dam towards the end of April. Subsequent dry conditions allowed the Mississippi River to slowly recede and drop below minor flood stage at the Winona Dam on May 5th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Volunteer observers reported quarter sized hail near Wabasha on the evening of May 6th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of heavy rain and anomalously warm temperatures melting a deeper than average snowpack during April resulted in moderate to major flooding at sites along the Upper Mississippi River in Minnesota. Initial minor flood stage was reached in mid April with near record crests towards the end of April. Drier conditions during the latter half of April and well below normal rainfall through May allowed the Upper Mississippi River to steadily recede, dropping below minor flood stage at sites in Minnesota during the beginning of May.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant upstream runoff from the combination of heavy rainfall and rapidly melting snow resulted in the sixth highest crest on record in Lake City towards the end of April. Subsequent dry conditions allowed the Mississippi River to slowly recede and drop below minor flood stage in Lake City on May 2nd.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The combination of heavy rainfall and rapidly melting snow resulted in the fourth highest crest on record in Winona towards the end of April. Subsequent dry conditions allowed the Mississippi River to slowly recede and drop below minor flood stage in Winona on May 7th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving storm system dumped heavy snow on portions of southeast Minnesota from March 9th into the 10th. Snowfall amounts up to 6 inches were reported across Olmsted and Houston Counties. The highest reported total was 6.6 inches near Hokah (Houston County) and Mound Prairie (Houston County) and 6.5 inches near Elba (Olmsted County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported that 5 to 6 inches of snow fell across Houston County. The highest reported total was 6.6 inches near Hokah and Mound Prairie.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of heavy rain and anomalously warm temperatures melting a deeper than average snowpack during April resulted in moderate to major flooding at sites along the Upper Mississippi River in Minnesota. Initial minor flood stage was reached in mid April with near record crests towards the end of April. Drier conditions during the latter half of April and well below normal rainfall through May allowed the Upper Mississippi River to steadily recede, dropping below minor flood stage at sites in Minnesota during the beginning of May.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The combination of heavy rainfall and rapidly melting snow resulted in the fourth highest crest on record in La Crescent towards the end of April. Subsequent dry conditions allowed the Mississippi River to slowly recede and drop below minor flood stage in La Crescent on May 5th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex storm system brought strong thunderstorms to parts of southeast Minnesota during the late afternoon of Friday, March 31, 2023. Up to nickel sized hail was reported in Greenleafton (Fillmore County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel sized hail was reported in Greenleafton, southwest of Preston.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rapid snowmelt from a few day period of well above normal temperatures focused on the 11-13th of April resulted in minor to locally moderate flooding through much of the Big Sioux River Basin. Some areas north of I-90 lost between 2 to 5 inches of snow water equivalent (SWE) from the first to second week of April, but antecedent drought conditions resulted in only a percentage of this water actually making it into the river system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Redwood River near Russell, MN rose above flood stage during the early morning of April 12th before cresting around 0.63 feet above flood stage (14.63 feet) on April 13th. The river then fell below flood stage on the evening of April 13th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon of May 06 a weak frontal boundary was draped across western Minnesota. Afternoon clearing allowed a few hundred Joules of MLCAPE to develop, and storm relative helicity increased along and north of the boundary. A few rotating thunderstorms developed and produced several brief, weak tornadoes, along with one report of quarter size hail. This was a low end severe weather event, with little impacts.||The only damage report was to an out building. Video shows part of the roof being blown off.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Video shows a tornado moving mainly east, and then lifting north/northeast across far western Renville county. There was minor damage to an outbuilding. The tornado was rated EF0 based on the damage in the video.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure moved from the Central Plains early morning on March 31 into the western Great Lakes by early morning April 1. The area was just on the south side of the more significant banded precipitation, and featured enough instability for a little thunder to mix in during the morning hours. Most precipitation started as rain or freezing rain, mixing with and changing to snow through the afternoon and early evening from west to east. Winds behind the system increased to a gusty 30 to 40 mph at the peak, with snowfall generally from 2 to 4 inches north of I-90 and west of the James River, as well as southwest Minnesota mainly north of Highway 14. The combination of wind and snowfall led to some near-blizzard conditions for a brief time during the late evening and early overnight hours of April 1.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A continuation of the winter weather event from March 31, total snow accumulation of 1.5 inches was reported at a CoCoRaHS site 8 miles southeast of Hendricks. Northerly winds reached gusts as high as 43 mph at Minnesota Road Weather Information System site MN028 near Lake Benton, with visibilities briefly one mile or less between 0000-0100CST.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A continuation of the winter weather event from March 31, total snow accumulation of 3.0 inches was reported at COOP site MNNM5 at Minneota, 2.5 inches at COOP site MMLM5 at Marshall, and 3.5 inches at a CoCoRaHS site 5 miles south of Cottonwood. Northerly winds reached gusts as high as 46 mph at Minnesota Road Weather Information System site MN156 near Taunton and 40 mph at Automated Weather Observation System site KMML at Marshall, with visibilities one mile or less between 2200CST March 31 and 0200CST April 1.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With steep near surface lapse rates and a diffuse boundary across southwest Minnesota, a quick spin-up developed during the updraft phase of a thunderstorm which resulted in a brief landspout.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A landspout quickly spun up around 2 miles northwest of Windom in an open field just north of the Windom Country Club.  The weak tornado moved just east of north and crossed 405th Street, where it glanced a farmstead and caused damage to shingles on an outbuilding. The tornado continued about a half mile across open fields before turning more northerly, and crossed 395th Street before lifting less than a quarter mile farther to the north. The maximum wind speed was 65 mph and the average path width was 20 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure deepened as it moved from eastern Colorado on April 4 to central Minnesota on April 5. Slightly warmer and drier air aloft east of the upper-level low track kept precipitation type as mainly light freezing rain and freezing drizzle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Periods of freezing drizzle, mixed briefly with snow, brought a light glaze of ice accumulations to Marshall, Tracy, and Minneota.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong wind gradient around a large upper level circulation resulted in widespread winds of 30 to 40 mph and gusts from 45 to 65 mph east of the James River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Northwest winds were sustained from 40 to 45 mph, with a peak gust of 58 mph at the Minnesota Road Weather Information System site MN028 1 mile west of Lake Benton.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail shredded leaves on trees.

Several low-top supercells developed and tracked along portions of the Minnesota Red River Valley. Portions of Norman, Polk, Becker, and Mahnomen counties saw multiple tornadoes. Large hail and damaging winds accompanied some of these storms, and flash flooding in some locations as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A period of strong winds and waves led to hazardous swimming conditions along the North Shore of Lake Superior the weekend of the 24th and 25th. Two cliff jumpers drowned in the waters near Black Beach in Silver Bay after jumping due to the waves and cold water temperatures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two 18 year old males died while cliff jumping at Black Beach in Silver Bay. The two jumped from the cliff just before 1:30PM CDT on the 25th and were seen struggling in the water before slipping beneath the waves. Waves at the time were in the 3 to 6 foot range with northeast winds gusting to 20 to 25 knots. Additionally the lake temperatures were in the lower 40s Fahrenheit. The Lake County Sheriff's office cited the winds, waves and water temperatures as contributing the drownings. Small Craft Advisories were in effect highlighting the hazardous conditions in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions spread across the Northland during the month of June with all areas seeing at least D0 Abnormally dry conditions by the end of the month. Some pockets of D2 Severe Drought began to appear during the middle of the month around the head of Lake Superior and in far southern areas of Crow Wing and Pine counties in northeastern Minnesota. Rainfall for the month of June was generally around 1 to 3 inches below normal aside from some parts of the Arrowhead that received above normal rainfall. Most areas on received around 50 percent of normal rainfall for the month leading to stress on crops and area waterways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Following record snowfall over the preceding winter, drought conditions quickly spread across the Northland during the spring. A small sliver of D2 Significant Drought was added to far southwestern Pine County with the update on the 13th and persisted through the remainder of the month. A small sliver of far northeastern Pine County was added to the D2 Severe Drought area with the update on the 20th. Rainfall deficits in excess of 2.5 inches were observed across much of Pine County which is about 50 to 70 percent of normal for the month of June.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Linear storms developed near a frontal boundary, moving across southeast South Dakota and into northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota early on June 24. Within an environment of shear, a strong low-level jet, and strong DCAPE, some storms produced severe-level winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed a large tree limb in End-O-Line Railroad Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak impulse dropped southward along the western side of a Great Lakes closed mid-level low, igniting isolated storms overnight.  Weak shear and evaporation within a low-level dry airmass resulted in localized damaging wind gusts with the strongest storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ten wooded power poles were snapped off by thunderstorm winds along a half-mile stretch along 850th Street west of Highway 71. Property damage costs are rough estimates.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak impulse dropped southward along the western side of a Great Lakes closed mid-level low, igniting isolated storms overnight.  Weak shear and evaporation within a low-level dry airmass resulted in localized damaging wind gusts with the strongest storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds uprooted a large cottonwood tree and tipped and damaged a grain auger.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stagnant pattern with weak easterly flow led to a diurnal development of thunderstorms, with one water-loaded storm collapsing with a burst of locally damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed up to 10 large trees in a grove.  A gazebo was blown at least 200 feet through a garage door and the winds displaced deck furniture up to 500 feet.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed a large tree limb.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Occasional thunderstorm wind gusts as high as 65 mph were measured at Minnesota Road Weather Information System site MN160 near Florence.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another in a series of jet streaks in northwest flow aloft initiated afternoon and early evening convection across eastern South Dakota, southwest Minnesota, and northwest Iowa. Several storms in an environment of 1500-2000 MLCAPE and around 40 knots of distributed deep-layer shear took on supercellular characteristics and produced hail up to baseball size in southwest Minnesota.

Another in a series of jet streaks in northwest flow aloft initiated afternoon and early evening convection across eastern South Dakota, southwest Minnesota, and northwest Iowa. Several storms in an environment of 1500-2000 MLCAPE and around 40 knots of distributed deep-layer shear took on supercellular characteristics and produced hail up to baseball size in southwest Minnesota.

Showers developed across northern Minnesota during the afternoon hours of the 17th as an upper trough rotated through the Northland. One of these showers produced a weak landspout in Orr with videos and photos showing it as it moved through. No damage was reported and it may have begun as a waterspout over adjacent Pelican Lake.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Landspout. Location: 48.054697N / 92.831284W. Video was received from an Orr fire department member via a local TV meteorologist showing a landspout moving through Orr. Some small debris was seen lofted at times along with the rotation followed by trees swaying heavily in the wind as the center of circulation passed over. Another video from a boat on Pelican Lake looking east at Orr showing rotation in the clouds aloft along with a ground circulation. The landspout may have started as a waterspout over Pelican Lake, but this was not evident in the videos received, only by the radar depiction of the parent shower. No damage was reported and the time and location were estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong high pressure and elevated near surface moisture resulted in a multi-day heat wave across southeast South Dakota, southwest Minnesota, northwest Iowa, and northeast Nebraska from July 25-28. With air temperatures rising into the 90s and low 100s and dewpoint temperatures in the 70s and low 80s, afternoon and evening heat indices generally peaked from 100 to 120 degrees with little overnight recovery. The most widespread opressive conditions occurred during the afternoons and evenings of July 26 and July 27.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Daily afternoon and evening heat indices ranged from 100 to 108 degrees with a maximum heat index of 108 degrees at the Remote Automatic Weather Station site at Pipestone National Monument on July 27.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong high pressure and elevated near surface moisture resulted in a multi-day heat wave across southeast South Dakota, southwest Minnesota, northwest Iowa, and northeast Nebraska from July 25-28. With air temperatures rising into the 90s and low 100s and dewpoint temperatures in the 70s and low 80s, afternoon and evening heat indices generally peaked from 100 to 120 degrees with little overnight recovery. The most widespread opressive conditions occurred during the afternoons and evenings of July 26 and July 27.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Daily afternoon and evening heat indices ranged from 100 to 110 degrees with a maximum heat index of 110 degrees at the Minnesota Department of Transportation site near Beaver Creek on July 27.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent shortwave diving south out of Manitoba kicked off storms that produced large hail across parts of the region. One storm during the early afternoon produced tennis ball size hail denting vehicles on Highway 38 in northern Itasca County. This storm continued to move to the south and moved through the Grand Rapids area periodically dropping hail stones an inch in diameter. Other storms that afternoon across the Minnesota Arrowhead produced quarter-sized hail in Tettegouche State Park.

Drought conditions rapidly worsened across the Northland during the month of July. After record snowfall in many areas over the winter, dry conditions took hold for the spring into the summer with rainfall deficits of 1 to 4 inches for the month across much of northeastern Minnesota.  D1 Moderate Drought quickly took hold across the area during the beginning of the month with D2 Severe Drought spreading into far western and southern areas as the month progressed. Area creeks and rivers dropped to low flow levels and crops began to suffer across much of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: July began with a small area of D1 Severe Drought across the far southern reaches of southern Aitkin County with D0 Abnormally Dry elsewhere across the county. Dry conditions over the month saw D2 Severe Drought introduced with the update on the 11th and then expanding northward across the remainder of the southern part of the county by 25th. Rainfall deficits for the month of 2 to 4 inches were observed across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another in a series of jet streaks in northwest flow aloft initiated afternoon and early evening convection across eastern South Dakota, southwest Minnesota, and northwest Iowa. Several storms in an environment of 1500-2000 MLCAPE and around 40 knots of distributed deep-layer shear took on supercellular characteristics and produced hail up to baseball size in southwest Minnesota.

Warm and humid conditions developed across northeastern Minnesota on the 27th ahead of a frontal boundary that stalled out across the region. Storms developed during the afternoon hours and were prolific hail producers and they moved through. Multiple reports of hail to the size of tennis balls were received. A few areas of wind damage were reported as well, primarily in the Duluth area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mature maple tree with a diameter of 10 inches was down on the northeast corner of the UMD campus.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moved across the Northland during the day on the 19th setting the stage for severe storms during the afternoon and evening across the region. The storms that developed were prolific hail producers with hail to the size of baseballs reported in northern Itasca County. A few isolated reported of tree damage were received, but hail was the primary threat from these storms. Additionally, lightning struck a barn that was over 100 years old starting a fire that completely destroyed the barn.

Two rounds of storms were seen late on the 9th ahead of a cold front dropping out of Canada. The first round developed during the evening hours across the Brainerd Lakes region following an afternoon of very warm and humid conditions. These storms quickly become strong but a lack of shear led to them quickly falling apart. A few storms were able to produce hail to the size of golf balls before weakening. Later in the evening storms developed across northern Minnesota ahead of the cold front and moved into the Iron Range region. These storms were mainly sub-severe, but a few did produce isolated damaging wind gusts leading to a few downed trees and branches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were reported down along Murphy Lake Road near Central Lakes. Trees were conifers that were snapped near the base. Power outages were reported in the area as well.

A few isolated reported of tree damage were received, but hail was the primary threat from these storms. Additionally, lightning struck a barn that was over 100 years old starting a fire that completely destroyed the barn.

Warm and humid conditions developed across northeastern Minnesota on the 27th ahead of a frontal boundary that stalled out across the region. Storms developed during the afternoon hours and were prolific hail producers and they moved through. Multiple reports of hail to the size of tennis balls were received. A few areas of wind damage were reported as well, primarily in the Duluth area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several medium-sized trees snapped near the base that fell onto power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of storms were seen late on the 9th ahead of a cold front dropping out of Canada. The first round developed during the evening hours across the Brainerd Lakes region following an afternoon of very warm and humid conditions. These storms quickly become strong but a lack of shear led to them quickly falling apart. A few storms were able to produce hail to the size of golf balls before weakening. Later in the evening storms developed across northern Minnesota ahead of the cold front and moved into the Iron Range region. These storms were mainly sub-severe, but a few did produce isolated damaging wind gusts leading to a few downed trees and branches.

A potent shortwave diving south out of Manitoba kicked off storms that produced large hail across parts of the region. One storm during the early afternoon produced tennis ball size hail denting vehicles on Highway 38 in northern Itasca County. This storm continued to move to the south and moved through the Grand Rapids area periodically dropping hail stones an inch in diameter. Other storms that afternoon across the Minnesota Arrowhead produced quarter-sized hail in Tettegouche State Park.
An axis of instability moved through NW MN, sparking off convection in the evening hours of August 1st. Large hail and damaging wind gusts were reported with these cells.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the early evening of the 4th, two swimmers were caught in a rip current while swimming along the Park Point Beach near the Duluth Aerial Lift Bridge. A passerby entered the water to rescue them and successfully did so. The swimmers did not require medical attention and were not injured.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two swimmers were rescued after being caught in a rip current along the Park Point beach near the Duluth Aerial Lift Bridge. The swimmers were two juvenile females that stated that they were swept out into the lake while swimming in waist to chest deep water. One of the swimmers said that she was wading in the waves and was instantly swept off of her feet. She began to be taken out into the lake. Her friend came into the water to save her and was also caught in the rip current. Upon arrival, Duluth Fire learned that the swimmers were rescued by a passerby. The rescuer was walking the beach with a companion and said that he heard the cry for help. He reported that one of the swimmers had mostly made it to shore. He quickly went for the second swimmer. Both of the swimmers were safely retrieved and examined by paramedics, but did not require medical care.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions rapidly worsened across the Northland during the month of July. After record snowfall in many areas over the winter, dry conditions took hold for the spring into the summer with rainfall deficits of 1 to 4 inches for the month across much of northeastern Minnesota.  D1 Moderate Drought quickly took hold across the area during the beginning of the month with D2 Severe Drought spreading into far western and southern areas as the month progressed. Area creeks and rivers dropped to low flow levels and crops began to suffer across much of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: July began with D2 Severe Drought across the far southeastern reaches of St. Louis County and the far eastern reaches of Carlton County with D1 Moderate Drought prevailing elsewhere. Some rain at the end of June into early July led to a brief period of improvement with the updates on the 4th and the 11th which saw a reduction in the coverage of D2 Severe Drought. However, rainfall became quite sparse starting around the middle of the month with D2 Severe Drought spreading across all of Carlton County with the update on the 25th and back into southeastern and far southern St. Louis County as well. Rainfall deficits  of 2 to 3 inches below normal for the month were observed across the region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front pushing southeast out of southern Manitoba and far western Ontario led to a few isolated thunderstorms across the Minnesota Borderlands during the afternoon of the 8th. Most of the storms remained below severe limits but a few were able to produce quarter-sized hail and some minor tree damage as they moved through.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail was mostly dime-sized but a few hailstones were quarter-sized.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An occluded low pressure system moved across the Upper Midwest during the afternoon and evening hours of the 11th. Storms formed in the warm sector ahead of the cold front south of a triple-point that moved through the Brainerd area. These storms were mainly wind-producing storms with multiple reports of tree damage received from the Brainerd Lakes area eastward into the I-35 corridor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few small branches were down along with pea-sized hail.

A heat dome to the south resulted in temperatures in the upper 90s and dewpoints in the upper 70s and low 80s. A temperature of 100 degrees and a dewpoint of 85 degrees generated a peak heat index value of 133 degrees on a personal weather station 8 miles north of Odessa, though no information on how this station has been cited is available. Across Big Stone County, the peak heat index exceeded 110 degrees at every weather station reported for the day.

Abundant moisture, relatively weak air flow, and a weak disturbance moving through the region produced scattered thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening of June 10. Water loaded storms produced locally heavy rainfall and some flooding. In addition, the developing phase of storms across southwest Minnesota resulted in very brief landspouts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief landspout touched down in an open field just east of 560th Avenue and north of Minnesota State Highway 30.  This tornado resulted in no visible damage and had no basis for rating.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and evening hours of the 16th across northeastern Minnesota ahead of a cold front. Daytime heating led to strong instability over the region and a strong low level jet provided ample wind shear for storms to work with. The storms didn't really get organized until they moved into northwest Wisconsin, but one storm did produce nickel-sized hail in Sandstone.

An isolated severe thunderstorm developed ahead of a cold front during the afternoon of the 25th. While the storm did not look that impressive on radar, it was able to mix down some strong winds that brought down a few trees and caused some damage in Esko.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree debris was partially blocking the Esko exit off Interstate 35. Additionally, two larger trees about 12 inches in diameter were down nearby.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought continued from July in NW MN. It worsened to D3 by mid August, with the D3 area covering 2 counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: D2 drought began in Eastern Marshall county on August 29th. It continued into the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak frontal boundary was draped east/west across the region. By mid-afternoon, scattered thunderstorms developed along this boundary. There was minimal instability, and minimal wind shear. As a result, storms were very pulse-like, or short-lived. A few of the stronger cores managed to produce damaging wind, with one report of severe hail. This was a low-end event, with a few sporadic severe weather reports. Some reports include:||Trees down east-southeast of West Union.|Trees down near North Branch.|Measured 60 mph gust at KMIC ASOS.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported a 20 inch diameter pine tree snapped, with a photo relayed via social media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions persisted across northeastern Minnesota during the month of August. Rainfall continued to be in short supply across much of the region with areas receiving only about 50 percent or less of normal rainfall for the month. Localized heavy rainfall during the beginning of the month in the Brainerd Lakes area led to some improvement, but with dry conditions over the remainder of the month, D3 Extreme Drought spread across the southern reaches of northeastern Minnesota by the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The month began with D2 Severe Drought across the far western portions of southern Itasca County. This area expanded a bit to the east with the update on the 22nd and encompassed roughly the western third of the southern part of the county. Very dry conditions were observed through the month with less than 25 percent of normal rainfall falling. Some areas in the central part of the county received less than 5 percent of normal rainfall for the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two waves of thunderstorms in central Minnesota produced significant wind damage. A brief EF-0 tornado occurred near Rockford, MN in Wright County during the first wave of storms. The strongest estimated thunderstorm gusts were around 75-80 mph given the nature of the observed wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A downburst hit a large area southeast and east of Buffalo, MN. The storm survey found a path that was just over four miles long and which broadened out as the downburst moved to the northeast. Hundreds of trees were downed, mostly to the northwest, north, and northeast. Some trees fell on sheds, garages, and vehicles. Multiple farm buildings suffered major damage. A large window in a house was blown inward. A double garage door was blown in and soffit from an adjacent barn was blown off. The last of the surveyed damage was to a park and farm on the southern shore of Beebe Lake. At the farm, winds blew in the south wall of a machine shed which landed on a combine. Portions of this shed were found nearly 300 yards to the northwest.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions persisted across northeastern Minnesota during the month of August. Rainfall continued to be in short supply across much of the region with areas receiving only about 50 percent or less of normal rainfall for the month. Localized heavy rainfall during the beginning of the month in the Brainerd Lakes area led to some improvement, but with dry conditions over the remainder of the month, D3 Extreme Drought spread across the southern reaches of northeastern Minnesota by the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Crow Wing County began the month in D2 Severe Drought but the central portion of the county was upgraded to D3 Extreme Drought with the update on the morning of the first. This area of D3 Extreme Drought was slowly reduced over the next two weeks and was completely gone by the 15th. The extreme southern portion of the county also improved to D1 Moderate Drought with this update as well. Continued dry conditions over the remainder of the month saw D2 Severe Drought return over the entire county by the 22nd with D3 Extreme Drought returning to the northern three-quarters of the county by the 29th. Rainfall amounts around 70 percent of normal were observed across the southern part of the county with the northern part seeing slightly above normal rainfall for the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface low pressure system tracked across the North Dakota/South Dakota border creating an environment supporting the threat for low top supercells in Southeastern North Dakota into adjacent West Central Minnesota. Several low-top supercells developed and tracked along portions of the Minnesota Red River Valley. Portions of Norman, Polk, Becker, and Mahnomen counties saw multiple tornadoes. Large hail and damaging winds accompanied some of these storms, and flash flooding in some locations as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter reported another touchdown near Beltrami MN. It occurred in open fields, which mitigated any damage. Therefore, the rating of EF-U has been assigned with estimated path width and length.

There was minimal instability, and minimal wind shear. As a result, storms were very pulse-like, or short-lived. A few of the stronger cores managed to produce damaging wind, with one report of severe hail. This was a low-end event, with a few sporadic severe weather reports. Some reports include:||Trees down east-southeast of West Union.|Trees down near North Branch.|Measured 60 mph gust at KMIC ASOS.

With a weak moisture convergence axis in place and 500-1000 J/kg MLCAPE in the presence of 40 knots of deep-layer shear, isolated multicell storms developed which produced marginally large hail during the very brief life cycle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few storms developed during the afternoon along a cold frontal boundary with up to 2000 J/kg available CAPE and 40 to 50 knots of effective shear. Rotating storms produced large hail, and isolated very large hail across southwest Minnesota.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another in a series of jet streaks in northwest flow aloft initiated afternoon and early evening convection across eastern South Dakota, southwest Minnesota, and northwest Iowa. Several storms in an environment of 1500-2000 MLCAPE and around 40 knots of distributed deep-layer shear took on supercellular characteristics and produced hail up to baseball size in southwest Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong to severe cluster of storms moved through central Minnesota the evening of July 13th following the Mississippi River Valley. As the storms crossed Wabasha and Winona counties late into the overnight, a rapid intensification resulted in very large hail falling within a narrow swath of approximately 10 miles between the two counties. Hail reports varied in size from ping pong ball near Plainview (Wabasha County) to tea cup near Altura (Winona County) and Lewiston (Winona County). Significant damage was reported to crops, automobiles, and housing structures near Altura.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported ping pong ball sized hail near Plainview.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed along a frontal boundary during the mid to late afternoon as convergence moved from far eastern South Dakota into southwest Minnesota. A decent amount of mid-level instability resulted in MLCAPE up to 1500 J/kg while deep-layer shear remained largely less than 30 kts. This environment favored storms that produced large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another in a series of jet streaks in northwest flow aloft initiated afternoon and early evening convection across eastern South Dakota, southwest Minnesota, and northwest Iowa. Several storms in an environment of 1500-2000 MLCAPE and around 40 knots of distributed deep-layer shear took on supercellular characteristics and produced hail up to baseball size in southwest Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Limited instability and shear led to rapidly developing and dissipating storms. The strongest storms briefly produced large hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak frontal boundary was draped east/west across the region. By mid-afternoon, scattered thunderstorms developed along this boundary. There was minimal instability, and minimal wind shear. As a result, storms were very pulse-like, or short-lived. A few of the stronger cores managed to produce damaging wind, with one report of severe hail. This was a low-end event, with a few sporadic severe weather reports. Some reports include:||Trees down east-southeast of West Union.|Trees down near North Branch.|Measured 60 mph gust at KMIC ASOS.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported that the hail had shredded some corn as seen in a photo that the spotter provided to NWS Twin Cities.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a strong mid-level jet streak pushed northeast ahead of an upper-level low pressure area in southwest South Dakota, isolated thunderstorms developed late afternoon in southwest Minnesota. While instability was somewhat limited at a maximum of around 1000 J/kg, a very low LCL and concentration of 0-1 km bulk shear supported the development of a brief tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spin up developed around one-half mile north-northeast of Leota. A storm chaser witnessed field debris lofted and circulated by the very brief funnel just west of Birkett Avenue. There were no damage indicators present to allow for a rating of this tornado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions persisted across northeastern Minnesota during the month of August. Rainfall continued to be in short supply across much of the region with areas receiving only about 50 percent or less of normal rainfall for the month. Localized heavy rainfall during the beginning of the month in the Brainerd Lakes area led to some improvement, but with dry conditions over the remainder of the month, D3 Extreme Drought spread across the southern reaches of northeastern Minnesota by the end of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Northern Aitkin County began the month with a mixture of D1 Moderate Drought across the northern half of the northern part of the county with D2 Severe Drought across the southern half. The area of D2 Severe Drought was expanded across the northwestern quarter of the northern half of the county with the update on the morning of the first.  Continued dry conditions over the remainder of the month saw D2 Severe Drought expanding across the much of the remainder of the northern half of the county with the update on the 22nd with D3 Extreme Drought being introduced across the southern half of the northern part of the county on the 29th. Rainfall amounts around 50 to 70 percent of normal were observed across the northern part of the county for the month.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pine County began the month with D2 Severe Drought across most of the county along with a small area of D1 Moderate Drought along the St. Croix River. The area of D2 Severe Drought was expanded across the remainder of the county with the update on the morning of the first.  Some improvement was noted with the update on the 15th as D1 Moderate Drought was added across the southwestern corner of the county. Continued dry conditions over the remainder of the month saw D2 Severe Drought expanding across the entire county once again with the update on the 22nd with D3 Extreme Drought being introduced across the northern quarter the county on the 29th. Rainfall amounts around 50 to 70 percent of normal were observed across the county for the month with the greatest deficits across the northern part of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed along a frontal boundary during the afternoon and early evening.  Stretching at the cloud base resulted in several reported funnels, with one brief tornado across southwest Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down around 1 mile west-southwest of Kinbrae, just south of 120th Street to the east of Town Avenue.  The tornado traveled northeast and crossed the road, where the tornado passed through a farmstead and removed a few metal panels from a farm outbuilding. After the tornado left a path across a corn field while it moved another one-half mile northeast, the tornado impacted a tree line and uprooted a large pine tree.  The tornado continued across open fields with a slightly more eastward turn, then lifted near the intersection of 110th Street and Ulrich Avenue.  The path was captured by a local resident with a drone, with an average path width of 40 yards. The tornado was rated an EF-0 with a maximum wind speed of 80 mph. Property damage costs are a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With a lingering upper-level trough across the area, a weak surface convergence axis and diurnal destabilization produced a few late afternoon thunderstorms across southwest Minnesota and into northwest Iowa in the evening. Stronger cells produced marginally large hail, and some damaging wind gusts with a strong sub-cloud dry layer.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pea size hail transitioned into quarter size after initial contact.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted through the Northland during the morning of the 16th. Strong southwesterly winds were observed across the region starting during the late morning hours and continued into the mid to late afternoon before turning northwesterly as a cold front passed through. The northwesterly winds were stronger than the southwesterly winds due to a katabatic component to the winds. Winds then diminished quickly by the mid-evening hours. Measured wind gusts as high as 65 mph were received along with reports of downed trees along the terrain ridge of the North Shore.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A period of northwest katabatic winds were reported along the North Shore during the late afternoon and early evening of the 16th. A maximum wind gust of 66 mph was recorded at the KGNA Bay of Grand Marais ASOS at 6:03 PM. A few reports of downed trees were received between 6 and 8PM along the terrain ridge along the North Shore.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of storms were seen late on the 9th ahead of a cold front dropping out of Canada. The first round developed during the evening hours across the Brainerd Lakes region following an afternoon of very warm and humid conditions. These storms quickly become strong but a lack of shear led to them quickly falling apart. A few storms were able to produce hail to the size of golf balls before weakening. Later in the evening storms developed across northern Minnesota ahead of the cold front and moved into the Iron Range region. These storms were mainly sub-severe, but a few did produce isolated damaging wind gusts leading to a few downed trees and branches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: D2 Severe Drought conditions persisted across parts of Koochiching, Itasca, Cass, Crow Wing and Aitkin counties during November. Lingering wet conditions from the end of October led to some slight improvement in D2 Severe Drought across Koochiching County during the first week of the month, but minimal changes were observed over the remainder of the month. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions persisted across northeastern Minnesota during the month of October, but significant improvement was seen. All areas of D3 Extreme Drought were removed during the first week of the month with significant reductions in areas of D2 Severe Drought by the end of the month as well. Rainfall was near to slightly below normal for the month, but was enough along with lingering wet conditions from September to lead to the improvement in October.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: D2 Severe Drought conditions persisted through the month across the southwestern corner of the county. Some improvement was seen from D1 Moderate Drought to D0 Abnormally Dry across the eastern part of the county, however. Rainfall for the month was around 1 to 2 inches above normal across the central portion of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Precipitation was between 175 percent and 300 percent of normal across southwest Minnesota during October.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought conditions improved to moderate (D1) conditions by mid-month. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

A few noteworthy snowfall totals were:||5.7 inches near Mora in Kanabec County.|4.8 inches near Rush City in Chisago County.|4.0 inches near Albertville in Wright County.|3.8 inches near Brooklyn Park in Hennepin County.|3.7 inches near Big Lake in Sherburne County.|3.5 inches near Bradford in Isanti County.|3.5 inches near Apple Valley in Dakota County.|3.5 inches near Lake Elmo in Washington County.|3.4 inches near Belle Prairie in Morrison County.|3.3 inches near Highland Park (St. Paul) in Ramsey County.|3.3 inches near Ham Lake in Anoka County.|3.0 inches near Long Prairie in Todd County.|3.0 inches near Isle in Mille Lacs County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A couple bands of snow moved through overnight and produced generally 1 to 3 inches of snow. Temperatures were below freezing, which led to icy roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intensifying storm system brought multiple snow bands through southeast Minnesota late on October 30th into October 31st. While overall accumulations were only 1 to 2 inches, the combination with winds gusting above 40 mph and increased snowfall rates resulted in rapidly deteriorating travel conditions. Resultant road conditions quickly became slippery to hazardous across much of southeast Minnesota, especially with snow initially melting and then refreezing on pavement into the early morning hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: While volunteer observers reported only 1 to 2 inches of snowfall from late on October 30th into the morning of October 31st, the combination with 30 mph wind gusts rapidly worsened road conditions. The slippery to hazardous travel conditions resulted in multiple slide offs reported across the county.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow late on October 30th into the morning of October 31st brought near an inch of snow for much of Rochester according to volunteer observer reports. Minnesota State Patrol reported snow covered roadways at 0753 CST when a head-on collision occurred on Minnesota State Highway 42 near the intersection with Rochester 65th Street Northeast. One person in a pickup truck was driving north collided with a car driving south with 4 people inside. One person within the car sustained life-threatening injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure passing the south of Lake Superior led to a lake-effect snow event that affected parts of both the North and South Shores of Lake Superior starting the evening of the 30th and continuing into the 31st. The lake-effect band affected parts of the North Shore first in the Two Harbors and Silver Bay areas before moving south and east over portions of Douglas and Bayfield counties along the South Shore later in the event. Localized snowfall totals of 4 to 8 inches were reported under the band.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall reports of 3 to 6 inches were received with a high report of 6.5 inches about 20 miles north of Two Harbors.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong elevated instability near a stalled frontal boundary allowed for a weak impulse to spark an isolated severe thunderstorm across southwest Minnesota, which produced a track of sporadic large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ground was covered by nickel to quarter size hail.

Ongoing abnormally dry conditions worsened drought severity through the first half of September for all counties in southeast Minnesota. Rainfall totals during this time remained under a half of an inch, up to 2 inches below normal. The result was exceptional drought (D4) being realized for a portion of southeast Minnesota by the middle of the month, including Mower County and much of Fillmore and southern Houston counties. Resultant topsoil moisture values were 70% below normal through the third week of September. Widespread impacts were reported from volunteer observers and broadcast media. Stunted corn, very dry pastures, and stressed fruit trees limited the growing season. The Minnesota Department of Natural Resources raised their drought response phase to restrictive (D4) towards the start of September, asking residents of southeast Minnesota to limit outdoor water usage by 25%. A series of storms through the second half of the month provided near 5 of rainfall for much of southeast Minnesota. Highest 24 hour rainfall reports from volunteer observers included 4.4 near Kasson (Dodge County), 3.92 north of Rochester (Olmsted County), and 3.7 near Harmony (Fillmore County). The resultant soil moisture content for the final week of September neared 20% above normal for much of the area. The increased rainfall totals also resulted in the removal of exceptional drought (D4) across southeast Minnesota for the final drought monitor issuance of September. Extreme drought (D3) persisted for 75% of the local area through the end of the month. The United States Department of Agriculture declared most of southeast Minnesota as primary or contiguous natural disaster areas due to the drought to provide financial assistance.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ongoing drier than normal conditions, comprised of 9 consecutive weeks of at least severe drought (D2), expanded to all of Winona County at the start of September. By September 11th, under a half of an inch of rain fell across Winona County, which was 0.5 to 1.5 inches below normal for the time period. The resultant topsoil moisture neared 50% below normal. A series of storms provided 3 to 4 inches of rainfall across Winona County during the second half of September, up to 2 inches above normal for the time period. This resulted in a downgrade of the severe drought (D2) to moderate drought (D1) for nearly the entire county towards the end of September. Due to the drought, the United States Department of Agriculture declared Winona County as a contiguous natural disaster area on September 1st, 7th, and 12th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed ahead of a cold front moving across northern Minnesota during the afternoon hours of the 5th. Storms along the Iron Range of northeastern Minnesota were quite strong and produced damaging winds from the Hibbing area northeast to the Isabella area. Widespread tree damage was observed along with concentrated corridors of more significant damage. NWS meteorologists performed a storm survey of the hardest hit areas near Aurora and Hoyt Lakes and found damage indicative of straight line winds in excess of 90 mph. Additional storms were seen elsewhere across the region that produced minor tree damage and marginally severe hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Photos were received showing four pine tree trunks that had been snapped about halfway up. The trees were estimated to be about one foot in diameter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions persisted across northeastern Minnesota to begin September with D3 Extreme Drought stretching from the Brainerd Lakes area into the I-35 corridor. Two heavy rainfall events impacted the region during the month leading to many areas receiving 200 to 300 percent of normal rainfall for the month, especially in areas seeing the worst of the drought conditions. This led to drought improvement across much of the region with D3 Extreme Drought becoming confined to northern Cass County by the end of the month as much of the North Shore and Arrowhead improved to D0 Abnormally Dry.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: D2 Severe Drought conditions were in place across far southwestern Koochiching County to begin the month. Rainfall for the month was roughly near normal across the county with far northwestern areas trending a bit below normal as much of the remainder of the county saw slightly above normal rainfall. Most if this rainfall fell over the latter half of the month. There was a slight expansion of the area of D2 Severe Drought across the southwestern quarter of the county with the update on the 5th followed by a reduction to just the far southwest corner with the update on the 26th. The remainder of the county remained in D1 Moderate Drought throughout the month.
