Roughly the western half of northern Itasca County was in D2 Severe Drought. Rainfall for the month was around a inch below normal across most of the area with the far northwestern corner seeing slightly above normal rainfall. With lingering dry conditions from August, D2 Severe Drought expanded across all of northern Itasca County with the update on the 5th. A very small area of D3 Extreme Drought remained in the far southwest corner of northern Itasca County adjacent to Lake Winnibigoshish. No additional changes were noted over the last few days of the month.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The month began with D3 Extreme Drought across all of Carlton County and extreme southeastern St. Louis County with D2 severe drought over far southern St. Louis County. Lingering dry conditions from August led to the expansion of D3 Extreme Drought far southern St. Louis County as D2 Severe Drought expanded into the remainder of southern St. Louis County. Additionally, an area of D4 Exceptional Drought was added across the southwestern half of Carlton County. Rainfall for the month was around 3 to 6 inches plus above normal with much of it occurring over the latter half of the month, primarily during two heavy rainfall events that led to flash flooding in the Duluth area and along parts of the North Shore. This led to a reduction in the area of D3 Extreme Drought followed by the complete removal of D3 Extreme Drought and D4 Exceptional Drought  by the update on the 26th. While much of Carlton County and far southwestern southern St. Louis County remained in D2 Severe Drought, the remainder of the area improved to D1 Moderate Drought. No additional changes were noted over the last few days of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A flash flood event occurred in the Duluth area during the late evening of September 11th into the early morning hours of the 12th. An area of thunderstorms developed over Duluth during the evening hours and continued to backbuild over the hillside for a few hours before another area of rainfall pushed in from the west and helped push the backbuilding storms out over the lake. Rainfall totals of 3 to 6 inches fell over a small area at the top of the Duluth hillside in the Chester Creek basin in the area around College of St. Scholastica. This sent a surge of water downstream on Chester Creek down the Duluth hillside just to the east of downtown. The creek is generally underground for most of its run down the hill to Lake Superior, but the volume of water overwhelmed the tunnels and led to fast-flowing water on the surface. This resulted in a a few rescues by the fire department as the water cut-off access to an apartment building. At the bottom of the hill, Interstate 35 runs through a series of tunnels and the runoff from Chester Creek collected in the tunnels leading to a closure of the highway for a few hours until the water subsided.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall from backbuilding thunderstorms over Duluth led to 2 to 5 inches of rainfall in 2-3 hours. As the water rushed down the hillside to Lake Superior, it scoured out gravel alleys and sent around 1,000 cubic yards of construction debris, including sand, dirt, rock and other materials, onto Superior Street at the base of the hill. Superior Street at First Avenue East was closed for much of the 12th as crews worked to remove the piles of debris using heavy machinery.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving area of occluded low pressure lingered over the Red River Valley of the North the 24th into the 25th. Waves of Gulf moisture streaming northward across the region led to a concentrated area of moderate to heavy rainfall that impacted areas between Duluth and Two Harbors starting late on the 23rd and continuing into the 24th. Additional moisture was contributed by Lake Superior along with extra lift from the terrain along the North Shore leading to rainfall totals of 4 to 7 inches from the Duluth area to around Silver Bay. Areas of flooding and flash flooding were reported through this corridor mainly affecting the Miller Creek, Tischer Creek, Schmidt Creek, French River, Knife River and Big Sucker River watersheds. Scoured and washed out roads and railroads tracks were the primary impacts from the flooding along with build up of debris along bridges from the high water.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rapidly flowing water was observed along Pitt Street in the Lakeside neighborhood of Duluth.

Tischer Creek was overwhelmed by the water across much of its run through Duluth. Water was backed up across a large portion of the Ridgeview Country Club damaging bridges along the course. Water was also flowing through neighborhoods across streets and open areas with water over a foot deep in places. The main affects were observed across the Woodland and Hunter's Park neighborhoods of Duluth.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving area of occluded low pressure lingered over the Red River Valley of the North the 24th into the 25th. Waves of Gulf moisture streaming northward across the region led to a concentrated area of moderate to heavy rainfall that impacted areas between Duluth and Two Harbors starting late on the 23rd and continuing into the 24th. Additional moisture was contributed by Lake Superior along with extra lift from the terrain along the North Shore leading to rainfall totals of 4 to 7 inches from the Duluth area to around Silver Bay. Areas of flooding and flash flooding were reported through this corridor mainly affecting the Miller Creek, Tischer Creek, Schmidt Creek, French River, Knife River and Big Sucker River watersheds. Scoured and washed out roads and railroads tracks were the primary impacts from the flooding along with build up of debris along bridges from the high water.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Amity Creek was out of its banks following 3 to 5 inches of rainfall. The surging water damaged a gazebo along the creek along with numerous pedestrian bridges covering them with logs and other detritus.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fast flowing water 3 to 6 inches deep was running across yards and West Palm Street just upstream from the beginning of Coffee Creek in the Duluth Heights neighborhood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A flash flood event occurred in the Duluth area during the late evening of September 11th into the early morning hours of the 12th. An area of thunderstorms developed over Duluth during the evening hours and continued to backbuild over the hillside for a few hours before another area of rainfall pushed in from the west and helped push the backbuilding storms out over the lake. Rainfall totals of 3 to 6 inches fell over a small area at the top of the Duluth hillside in the Chester Creek basin in the area around College of St. Scholastica. This sent a surge of water downstream on Chester Creek down the Duluth hillside just to the east of downtown. The creek is generally underground for most of its run down the hill to Lake Superior, but the volume of water overwhelmed the tunnels and led to fast-flowing water on the surface. This resulted in a a few rescues by the fire department as the water cut-off access to an apartment building. At the bottom of the hill, Interstate 35 runs through a series of tunnels and the runoff from Chester Creek collected in the tunnels leading to a closure of the highway for a few hours until the water subsided.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms over parts of Duluth dropped upwards of 3 to 6 inches of rain over a localized area focused on the Chester Creek basin over a period of 2 to 3 hours. The highest rainfall totals were located at the top of the hill in the College of St. Scholastica area. Flash flooding was reported upstream from this location along Arrowhead Road where 3 to 6 inches of water was flowing over the road where Chester Creek passed under the road. As the surge of water flowed downstream, it damaged walkways along the creek and sent 2 to 5 inches of water onto side streets and Kenwood Avenue. Chester Creek runs through tunnels for much of its trip down the Duluth hillside and this volume of water overwhelmed the tunnels sending a large stream of rapidly flowing water 3 to 10 inches deep down the hill. This affected a few apartment buildings in the vicinity of 7th Avenue and 3rd Street which required a few rescues/evacuations of lower level residents and pets in the buildings by the fire department. Interstate 35 runs through tunnels underground at the base of the hill and the water surging down the hillside began to collect in the tunnels. Standing and flowing water in the tunnels resulted in the closure of the tunnels for a period. Water of at least 4 to 6 inches in depth was covering the southbound lanes in spots. The water receded during the early morning hours of the 12th with cleanup occurring over the remainder of the week.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The heavy rainfall led to Miller Creek spilling out of its banks and flooding parking lots in the vicinity of Maple Grove Road and Mall Drive in the Miller Hill Mall area. The water was around 6 inches deep or deeper and was completely surrounding buildings in the area. Portions of Mall Drive between Maple Grove and Haines roads were also under water from Miller Creek as well. Due to poor drainage, the flooding lasted into the evening hours before receding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe Drought (D2) lingered in portions of west central Minnesota to start the month of September. Near to above normal precipitation resulted in a downgrade to D0 and D1 conditions in Big Stone county by September 26th, with the COOP observers in Browns Valley and Ortonville measuring 2.4 and 1.23 inches of precipitation respectively.

A slow moving area of occluded low pressure lingered over the Red River Valley of the North the 24th into the 25th. Waves of Gulf moisture streaming northward across the region led to a concentrated area of moderate to heavy rainfall that impacted areas between Duluth and Two Harbors starting late on the 23rd and continuing into the 24th. Additional moisture was contributed by Lake Superior along with extra lift from the terrain along the North Shore leading to rainfall totals of 4 to 7 inches from the Duluth area to around Silver Bay. Areas of flooding and flash flooding were reported through this corridor mainly affecting the Miller Creek, Tischer Creek, Schmidt Creek, French River, Knife River and Big Sucker River watersheds. Scoured and washed out roads and railroads tracks were the primary impacts from the flooding along with build up of debris along bridges from the high water.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall amounts of 5 to 7 inches were recorded across the Schmidt Creek basin on the 23rd and 24th with the vast majority of the rainfall occurring on the 24th. This amount of water was significantly more than the creek could handle and issues began near the headwaters. A small culvert carrying the creek under Pioneer Junction Road failed starting with a sinkhole in the road before the fast moving waters completely scoured a channel through the road. Further downstream just after passing under Minnesota Highway 61, the rapidly flowing water encountered another too small culvert that carried the creek under the tracks of the North Shore Scenic Railroad. The water proceeded to scour out this culvert as well exposing a bridge that was originally built by the railroad at this location over 100 years prior. An NSSR train had passed over the bridge about an hour before the culvert failed and noted the developing washout. The train was then stranded in Two Harbors until the washout could be repaired a few days later.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ongoing abnormally dry conditions worsened drought severity through the first half of September for all counties in southeast Minnesota. Rainfall totals during this time remained under a half of an inch, up to 2 inches below normal. The result was exceptional drought (D4) being realized for a portion of southeast Minnesota by the middle of the month, including Mower County and much of Fillmore and southern Houston counties. Resultant topsoil moisture values were 70% below normal through the third week of September. Widespread impacts were reported from volunteer observers and broadcast media. Stunted corn, very dry pastures, and stressed fruit trees limited the growing season. The Minnesota Department of Natural Resources raised their drought response phase to restrictive (D4) towards the start of September, asking residents of southeast Minnesota to limit outdoor water usage by 25%. A series of storms through the second half of the month provided near 5 of rainfall for much of southeast Minnesota. Highest 24 hour rainfall reports from volunteer observers included 4.4 near Kasson (Dodge County), 3.92 north of Rochester (Olmsted County), and 3.7 near Harmony (Fillmore County). The resultant soil moisture content for the final week of September neared 20% above normal for much of the area. The increased rainfall totals also resulted in the removal of exceptional drought (D4) across southeast Minnesota for the final drought monitor issuance of September. Extreme drought (D3) persisted for 75% of the local area through the end of the month. The United States Department of Agriculture declared most of southeast Minnesota as primary or contiguous natural disaster areas due to the drought to provide financial assistance.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ongoing drier than normal conditions, comprised of 7 consecutive weeks of at least extreme drought (D3), expanded across all of Dodge County through September. By September 11th, less than a half inch of rain fell, more than inch below normal for the time period. The resultant topsoil moisture content was 50 to 70% below normal. A series of storms provided approximately 3 inches of rainfall during the second half of September, up to 2 inches above normal for the time period. These above normal rainfall totals resulted in downgrade of the extreme drought (D3) to severe drought (D2) for nearly all of Dodge County by the end of the month. The United States Department of Agriculture declared Dodge County as a contiguous natural disaster area due to the drought on September 7th and 22nd due to the ongoing drought.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed ahead of a cold front moving across northern Minnesota during the afternoon hours of the 5th. Storms along the Iron Range of northeastern Minnesota were quite strong and produced damaging winds from the Hibbing area northeast to the Isabella area. Widespread tree damage was observed along with concentrated corridors of more significant damage. NWS meteorologists performed a storm survey of the hardest hit areas near Aurora and Hoyt Lakes and found damage indicative of straight line winds in excess of 90 mph. Additional storms were seen elsewhere across the region that produced minor tree damage and marginally severe hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were uprooted or snapped off along Bass Lake Road. One of the trees fell onto an RV causing extensive damage to the RV.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Decaying thunderstorm caused a large tree to fall on an unoccupied vehicle in the city of Atwater. The photo was relayed by the emergency manager, and time was estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed in the afternoon of July 24, 2023 and produced isolated large hail and localized downburst winds across central and eastern Minnesota. The largest hail occurred in Dakota County, where golf ball size hail was observed near Hampton, along with wind damage near Rosemount.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees down on powerlines in Rosemont.  A construction trailer occupied by one person flipped over onto a vehicle off County Road 42, and the person was uninjured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Long-standing abnormally dry conditions, comprised of 6 to 10 inches below normal rainfall totals from June through September, slightly improved for parts of southeast Minnesota through October. Multiple storms provided 2 to 5 inches of rainfall which was near to slightly above normal. Resultant drought severity retreated south with extreme drought (D3) persisting along the Iowa border and severe drought (D2) just to the north.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Long-standing drier than normal conditions, comprised of 13 weeks of at least severe drought (D2), slightly eased in parts of Mower County through October. While several storms provided monthly rainfall totals up to two inches above normal, the ongoing rainfall deficit since summer allowed severe (D2) to extreme (D3) drought conditions to persist. From initial severe drought (D2) issuance on July 4th to early October, 5 to 8 inches of precipitation fell, approximately 6 inches below normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ongoing abnormally dry conditions worsened drought severity through the first half of September for all counties in southeast Minnesota. Rainfall totals during this time remained under a half of an inch, up to 2 inches below normal. The result was exceptional drought (D4) being realized for a portion of southeast Minnesota by the middle of the month, including Mower County and much of Fillmore and southern Houston counties. Resultant topsoil moisture values were 70% below normal through the third week of September. Widespread impacts were reported from volunteer observers and broadcast media. Stunted corn, very dry pastures, and stressed fruit trees limited the growing season. The Minnesota Department of Natural Resources raised their drought response phase to restrictive (D4) towards the start of September, asking residents of southeast Minnesota to limit outdoor water usage by 25%. A series of storms through the second half of the month provided near 5 of rainfall for much of southeast Minnesota. Highest 24 hour rainfall reports from volunteer observers included 4.4 near Kasson (Dodge County), 3.92 north of Rochester (Olmsted County), and 3.7 near Harmony (Fillmore County). The resultant soil moisture content for the final week of September neared 20% above normal for much of the area. The increased rainfall totals also resulted in the removal of exceptional drought (D4) across southeast Minnesota for the final drought monitor issuance of September. Extreme drought (D3) persisted for 75% of the local area through the end of the month. The United States Department of Agriculture declared most of southeast Minnesota as primary or contiguous natural disaster areas due to the drought to provide financial assistance.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ongoing drier than normal conditions, comprised of 9 consecutive weeks of at least severe drought (D2), expanded to encompass nearly all of Wabasha County through the middle of September. By September 11th, under a tenth of an inch of rain fell across Wabasha County, more than an inch below normal for the time period. The resultant topsoil moisture dropped to 50 to 70% below normal. A series of storms provided around 5 inches of rainfall across Wabasha County during the second half of September, near 3 inches above normal in spots for the time period. This resulted in removal of severe drought (D2) towards the end of September for nearly all of Wabasha County, replaced by moderate drought (D1). The United States Department of Agriculture declared Wabasha County as a primary natural disaster area on September 1st. Reported impacts included a reduction in apple size at an orchard in Elgin as well as failed squash crops without necessary irrigation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed ahead of a cold front moving across northern Minnesota during the afternoon hours of the 5th. Storms along the Iron Range of northeastern Minnesota were quite strong and produced damaging winds from the Hibbing area northeast to the Isabella area. Widespread tree damage was observed along with concentrated corridors of more significant damage. NWS meteorologists performed a storm survey of the hardest hit areas near Aurora and Hoyt Lakes and found damage indicative of straight line winds in excess of 90 mph. Additional storms were seen elsewhere across the region that produced minor tree damage and marginally severe hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: West central Minnesota experienced unusual heat and humidity for early September. On September 3rd, the heat index at the Minnesota DOT mesonet station reached 107 degrees with the high temperature topping out at 100 degrees.

This event lasted for several days, and up to an inch of ice accretion was reported in several counties across northwestern Minnesota. Numerous locations were without power in Minnesota Lakes Country, along with slick travel conditions lasting long after the precipitation stopped falling.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reports 0.25 inches of ice accumulation in the Horace and Fargo areas via social media. Storm total ice.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex storm impacted the Northern Plains in the days leading up to and through the Christmas holiday with widespread precipitation, wind, and hazardous conditions.  The storm began as rain during the afternoon and evening of December 23, briefly pausing before a second round of rainfall surged northward early on December 24.  Cooler temperatures slipped in near the surface by early Christmas Day, and this caused a widespread transition to freezing rain with ice accumulation varying from one-tenth to one-half inch, greatest near the crest of the Buffalo Ridge as warmer air wrapped into the area from the south and east within the dry slot. As the storm wound up and exited on December 26, colder air and wrap-around moisture brought up to one-half inch of light snowfall. ||Several long-term NWS Cooperative Observer locations in southwest Minnesota recorded the highest 3-day precipitation total on record for December including:||Minneota          2.75|Marshall           2.52|Lake Wilson      2.00|Pipestone         1.92|Lakefield 2 NE  1.69|Windom           1.51.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A period of freezing rain accumulated ice to one-quarter inch in Marshall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A system ramped up in the Red River Valley the 8th and 9th, with wind spread 3 to 5 inches of snow, a couple reports of 6 and 7 inches combined with winds gusting up to 50 mph in northwestern Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reports 6 inches of storm total snow in Middle River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Long-standing abnormally dry conditions from April 2023 through December resulted in no changes to severe (D2) and extreme (D3) drought in southeast Minnesota in December. From initial severe drought (D2) issuance in July 2023 to the onset of December, 10 to 15 of total rainfall was up to 10 below normal. While monthly total rainfall of 1 in December was only slightly below normal, the long-standing abnormally dry conditions resulted in continuation of the severe (D2) to extreme (drought). Well above normal temperatures in December, about 10 degrees above normal, further perpetuated soil moisture concerns. Broadcast media reported stunted growth and financial impacts for Christmas tree farms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) to extreme (D3) drought persisted across most of Mower County through December. While monthly rainfall near 1 was only slightly below normal, the long-standing abnormally dry conditions, 8 to 12 below normal, resulted in the severe (D2) to extreme (D3) drought persisting.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: D2 Severe Drought conditions persisted across parts of Koochiching, Itasca, Cass, Crow Wing and Aitkin counties during December. Little change in the area of D2 Severe Drought was observed through the month with only a slight reduction in area during the middle of the month. Precipitation for the month ended up near to above normal over most of the area thanks to a warm storm that moved through the region during the Christmas holiday period bringing rainfall to most areas. The rainfall was not enough, however, to make much of a difference in the overall dry conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: D2 Severe Drought conditions persisted through the month across the southwestern corner of the county. Precipitation for the month was within an inch of normal across the county with northern areas trending slightly below normal with southern areas slightly above normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Areas of dense fog resulted in mist and drizzle in southeast Minnesota on December 16th. Automated weather observation stations reported above freezing surface temperatures and minimum visibilities of 1/4 statute miles at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minnesota State Patrol reported an automobile accident along Minnesota State Highway 14 in Claremont Township on the afternoon of December 16th. Nearby automated weather observation stations reported rain, mist, and dense fog through much of the day. The accident report filed by the Minnesota State Patrol on December 16th classified the road conditions as wet.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice accumulation up to two tenths of an inch was reported at Worthington which brought down several smaller branches in town. Ice accumulation less than a quarter inch was widespread across the county with slick roads until temperatures rose into the 30s during the late afternoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A system ramped up in the Red River Valley the 8th and 9th, with wind spread 3 to 5 inches of snow, a couple reports of 6 and 7 inches combined with winds gusting up to 50 mph in northwestern Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Crookston AWOS measured winds gusting in excess of 40mph and up to visibility of a quarter mile or less.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: D2 Severe Drought conditions persisted across parts of Koochiching, Itasca, Cass, Crow Wing and Aitkin counties during December. Little change in the area of D2 Severe Drought was observed through the month with only a slight reduction in area during the middle of the month. Precipitation for the month ended up near to above normal over most of the area thanks to a warm storm that moved through the region during the Christmas holiday period bringing rainfall to most areas. The rainfall was not enough, however, to make much of a difference in the overall dry conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: D2 Severe Drought conditions persisted across all of northern Cass County for the duration of the month. Only a very small area of improvement was seen with the update on the 26th east of Outing. Precipitation was around 1 to 3 inches above normal for the month thanks to a wet system around the Christmas holiday that was mainly rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mix of precipitation moved through southeast Minnesota from late on December 4th through the morning of December 5th. Overall snow accumulations remained light across the area from a trace to 0.5. The timing of accumulating snowfall resulted in slippery road conditions for the morning commute.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minnesota State Patrol reported a pick-up truck was travelling east on Interstate 90 near Windom Township when their vehicle left the roadway and rolled. The driver was the only occupant and sustained life-threatening injuries. The nearest automated weather observation station reported mist and snow with below freezing surface temperatures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mix of freezing drizzle and light snow traversed southeast from late on December 30th in the early morning hours of New Year's Eve. Increased winds and below freezing temperatures during the afternoon hours of December 30th preceded initial freezing drizzle and resulted in rapid freezing on roadways. Multiple slide offs were reported across southeast Minnesota due the the rapid deterioration of road conditions including along Interstate 90, Minnesota State Highway 218 (Mower County), and Minnesota State Highway 43 (Winona County). The icy road conditions also resulted in Rochester (Olmsted County) Public Transit ceasing services on the evening of December 30th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minnesota State Patrol reported a rollover as an automobile left the roadway on Minnesota State Highway 43 in Hart Township. The accident report disclosed snow/ice covered roadways at the time of the accident.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow squalls impacted portions of the Duluth area in northeast Minnesota on December 17, 2022, as a quick moving cold front moved across the Northland during the evening hours. One squall moved through the Duluth area and brought heavy snow and very low visibilities that were very brief but impactful.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow Squall. Visibility dropped to one quarter of a mile in heavy snow at the KDLH Duluth Airport for 3 minutes. Winds were sustained at 20 mph and winds gusting to 32 mph as the squall moved through.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Above normal temperatures and low level moisture led to areas of freezing drizzle the morning of the 30th across northeastern Minnesota. The freezing drizzle caused slick and hazardous road conditions during this time and numerous other reports of spinouts, car crashes and cars in ditches were noted. In one crash, a driver lost control on the ice covered road, fishtailed, crossed the center line and collided with an oncoming vehicle before rolling and being ejected from the vehicle leading to significant injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing drizzle caused slick and hazardous road conditions during the early morning hours of the 30th. A driver lost control on the ice covered road near Hibbing just after 6AM. The vehicle fishtailed, crossed the center line and collided with an oncoming vehicle before leaving the road and rolling with the driver being ejected from the vehicle. The driver was not wearing a seatbelt and was transported to a regional hospital with serious, life-threatening injuries. The other driver was treated on scene and released. This was the most serious incident reported that morning among numerous reports of spinouts, wrecks and vehicles in ditches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of storms were seen late on the 9th ahead of a cold front dropping out of Canada. The first round developed during the evening hours across the Brainerd Lakes region following an afternoon of very warm and humid conditions. These storms quickly become strong but a lack of shear led to them quickly falling apart. A few storms were able to produce hail to the size of golf balls before weakening. Later in the evening storms developed across northern Minnesota ahead of the cold front and moved into the Iron Range region. These storms were mainly sub-severe, but a few did produce isolated damaging wind gusts leading to a few downed trees and branches.

A line of thunderstorms moved across parts of central and eastern Minnesota on the evening of May 30th. Several special weather statements were issued through the course of the afternoon with potential threats of gusty winds from 30-40 mph and hail up to the size of nickels. Only one report of penny size hail was reported one mile SSE of Lester Prairie, MN.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public report of penny size hail was reported 1 miles SSE of Lester Prairie, MN.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A southward advancing cold front over central and southern Minnesota generated a line of pulse or small, short-lived thunderstorms. Most storms from this episode remained sub-severe and produce brief gusty winds, localized heavy rain, and small hail. One strong storm that developed in southern Wright County and advanced into eastern McLeod County produced 68 mph wind gusts which was measured by a mesonet station 5 miles SSW of Waverly, MN. This storm also had reports of trees and power lines down in the city of Howard Lake, MN and quarter sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local law enforcement and trained spotters reported numerous trees and power lines down in the city of Howard Lake, MN, but no reported injuries or fatalities. A mesonet station just northeast of Lake Mary (5 miles south-southwest of Waverly) measured 68 mph. Also 4 miles SSW of Waverly, MN, a trained spotter reported damage to an agricultural field where crops appeared to have suffered wind and hail damage. Strong winds caused major damage to multiple barns on a farmstead. The strong thunderstorm continued into far northeastern McLeod County.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: This event is a continuation of the strong thunderstorm that developed into Wright County and traveled into far northeastern McLeod County. Emergency Management reported numerous large limbs and two trees that were brought down by strong wind gusts and blocked roads in Winsted.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail just east of Madelia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving system produced thunderstorms and heavy rainfall as result from a Trough of Warm Air Aloft (TROWAL) region of an upper low over South Central Minnesota. Due to the prolonged duration of heavy rainfall across the region, some localities experienced localized urban flooding. Brown County Emergency Management reported feet of water in the city of Comfrey, MN. ||Overall, the highest observed rainfall totals occurred in south central Minnesota:|8 miles N of Butterfield (Watonwan): 4.79 inches of rainfall.|1 mile SW of New Ulm (Brown): 4.64 inches of rainfall. |St. Peter (Nicollet): 4.43 inches of rainfall.|1 mile SW of Sleepy Eye (Brown): 4.39 inches of rainfall. |5 miles SE of Searles (Brown): 4.36 inches of rainfall.|8 miles N of St. James (Watonwan): 3.98 inches of rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Brown County Emergency Manager reported that ���feet��� of water was observed in the City of Comfrey, MN (Brown County) due to repeated and prolonged periods of heavy rainfall. Sandbagging efforts were undertaken in order to protect area residential basements from flooding. Approximately ten residents from a senior citizen townhome community were evacuated. Law enforcement reported that CR-17 through Comfrey had to be closed due to flooding. Additionally, the intersection of CR-5 and 110th St three miles west of Comfrey was also flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent shortwave diving south out of Manitoba kicked off storms that produced large hail across parts of the region. One storm during the early afternoon produced tennis ball size hail denting vehicles on Highway 38 in northern Itasca County. This storm continued to move to the south and moved through the Grand Rapids area periodically dropping hail stones an inch in diameter. Other storms that afternoon across the Minnesota Arrowhead produced quarter-sized hail in Tettegouche State Park.

Warm and humid conditions developed across northeastern Minnesota on the 27th ahead of a frontal boundary that stalled out across the region. Storms developed during the afternoon hours and were prolific hail producers and they moved through. Multiple reports of hail to the size of tennis balls were received. A few areas of wind damage were reported as well, primarily in the Duluth area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving system produced thunderstorms and heavy precipitation in the Trough of Warm Air Aloft (TROWAL) region of an upper low over South Central Minnesota. Due to the prolonged duration of heavy rainfall across the region, some localities experienced localized urban flash flooding. Waseca Emergency Managment reported street and small stream flooding 3 Miles NNW of New Richland, MN as well as in town. ||Overall, the highest observed rainfall totals occurred in south central Minnesota:|St. James, MN (Watonwan): 2.90 inches of rainfall.|Madelia, MN (Watonwan): 2.58 inches of rainfall.|Hanska, MN (Brown): 2.49 inches of rainfall.|3 miles SE of Cobden (Brown): 2.42 inches of rainfall. |1 mile NW of St. James: (Watonwan): 2.15 inches of rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency Management in Waseca County reported that street and small stream flooding was occurring in and about three miles north of New Richland, MN. There were also reports of numerous structures and homes with flooded basements. Approximately two miles north of New Richland, a trained spotter reported that heavy rains were allowing Boot Creek to flow about six feet out of its banks and it started to run over 120th Street (Paved elevated road). Approximately three miles north of New Richland, MN, heavy rainfall caused flooding of a roadway which cut off access to a household's driveway. New Richland City Administration confirmed that flash flood waters receded within 24 hours and no longer posed a threat to public safety.

Other storms that afternoon across the Minnesota Arrowhead produced quarter-sized hail in Tettegouche State Park.

On August 11, 2023 an unstable air mass with ample deep layer shear supported numerous supercell thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening. These storms produced widespread large hail, along with some damaging wind. A few reports were for greater than 2 inch hail. This event was categorized as a billion dollar disaster in Minnesota by the National Centers for Environmental Information (NCEI).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A picture of hail next to a ruler on social media showed hail slightly larger than 3 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface low pressure system tracked across the North Dakota/South Dakota border creating an environment supporting the threat for low top supercells in Southeastern North Dakota into adjacent West Central Minnesota. Several low-top supercells developed and tracked along portions of the Minnesota Red River Valley. Portions of Norman, Polk, Becker, and Mahnomen counties saw multiple tornadoes. Large hail and damaging winds accompanied some of these storms, and flash flooding in some locations as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This is a tornado that continued into Polk county from Norman Co. It moved into an open fields, which mitigated any damage and quickly diminished. Therefore, the rating of EF-U has been assigned with an estimated path width and length.

This event was categorized as a billion dollar disaster in Minnesota by the National Centers for Environmental Information (NCEI).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A picture was shared on social media of hail next to a baseball. The hail was slightly smaller than the baseball.

Excessive Heat occurred through much of Central and Southern MN through the afternoon of August 22nd, 2023. A warm front stalled over the region which allowed for a continued supply of warm moist air into the region. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface low pressure system tracked across the North Dakota/South Dakota border creating an environment supporting the threat for low top supercells in Southeastern North Dakota into adjacent West Central Minnesota. Several low-top supercells developed and tracked along portions of the Minnesota Red River Valley. Portions of Norman, Polk, Becker, and Mahnomen counties saw multiple tornadoes. Large hail and damaging winds accompanied some of these storms, and flash flooding in some locations as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Norman county Emergency Manager confirmed tornado touchdown east of Shelly, heading N-NW. This tornado lifted across the county line into Polk county. It occurred in open fields, which mitigated any damage. Therefore, the rating of EF-U has been assigned with an estimated path width and length.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe storms moved through southeast Minnesota during the evening of August 11th and produced winds in excess of 60 mph as well as quarter sized hail near Rochester (Olmsted County). Broadcast media reported power outages in the City of Winona (Winona County) and Claremont (Dodge County) from fallen trees on power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media reported power outages in the City of Winona.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions worsened across southeast Minnesota during the month of August. Monthly rainfall totals ranged from a half inch to 2 inches, which was 2 to 4 inches below normal. At the start of August, approximately 80 percent of southeast Minnesota was in at least severe drought (D2), with nearly 30 percent of the area in extreme drought (D3). By the end of August, with nearly 90 percent of southeast Minnesota in at least severe drought (D2), extreme drought (D3) conditions were impacting nearly 70 percent of the area. The United States Department of Agriculture declared most of southeast Minnesota as primary or contiguous natural disaster areas due to the drought to provide financial assistance.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ongoing drier than normal conditions, comprised of 5 consecutive weeks of at least severe drought (D2), spread and worsened in Winona County through August. During the first half of August, 1 to 1.5 inches of rain fell, initially preventing any spread of severe drought (D2) conditions that impacted the southwestern 40%. Under a third on an inch of rain fell during the second half of August, less than 10 percent of normal for the time period, resulting in extreme drought (D3) being realized for roughly 15 percent of Winona County (98 square miles). The United States Department of Agriculture declared Winona County as a primary disaster area due to the drought on August 10th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wide area of storms from the Central to Northern Plains initiated during the afternoon of June 2nd. A cluster of storms in the afternoon moved from Iowa into southeast Minnesota and resulted in severe winds that snapped a large tree near Byron (Olmsted County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public provided a picture of a large tree snapped and blown over via social media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: While parts of the 2023 summer had warm to hot temperatures, it took until middle to late August for the combination of hot temperatures and higher humidity levels to build a heat wave. Excessive heat advisories and warnings were initially issued on Tuesday, August 22nd and lasted through Thursday, August 24th. Heat indices reached and exceeded 100 degrees each day, with overall highest heat indices occurring on Wednesday, August 23rd. During this time, maximum daytime heat indices reached 118 degrees at Rochester (Olmsted County), Hokah (Houston County), and La Crescent (Winona County). Many sites in southeast Minnesota recorded heat indices of 105 to 120 degrees over the three days from August 22nd to August 24th. The excessive heat resulted in cancelled events in Rochester (Olmsted County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The combination of anomalously high temperatures and humidity on Tuesday, August 22nd and Wednesday, August 23rd resulted in maximum heat indices of 112 to 117 degrees in parts of Wabasha County. The maximum heat index reached 115 degrees on Tuesday and 117 degrees on Wednesday at the automated weather observation station in Lake City.

On the late evening of July 25, a supercell developed in the eastern Dakotas and moved into west central Minnesota. This supercell grew upscale into a small bow echo as it moved east/southeast into central and eventually eastern Minnesota during the overnight hours of the 25th into the 26th. The storm produced a damage swath about 1 to 2 counties wide, with some enhanced damage from near Stevens County down through the Stearns/Kandiyohi county line. There was also a brief EF0 tornado near New London.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Brooten fire department reported wind gusts of 50 to 60 mph about five miles south of Brooten.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Prolonged hot and humid conditions coupled with dry mid-levels in the atmosphere, led to extreme amounts of mixed-layer convective available potential energy (MLCAPE). The existence of deep layer shear allowed for numerous strong updrafts to develop across East Central MN with the primary hazard being hail in the afternoon hours. Later that evening in Southwestern MN, a surface boundary would converge on a cold pool which then would produce a line of thunderstorms capable of producing damaging wind gusts.||Hail measurements ranged between penny (0.75 inch) to Hen-Egg (2.0 inch) size. The highest measured hail was reported in the city of Morgan, MN or 13 miles SE of Redwood Falls located in Redwood County. ||Strong winds uprooted trees, broke large tree branches across much of the region affected by these storms. The areas with the most damage occurred in Chisago County as well as a line from Nicollet to Waseca Counties. The strongest gust (57 mph) was recorded at 11:56 PM at the Mankato Regional Airport.

On the late evening of July 25, a supercell developed in the eastern Dakotas and moved into west central Minnesota. This supercell grew upscale into a small bow echo as it moved east/southeast into central and eventually eastern Minnesota during the overnight hours of the 25th into the 26th. The storm produced a damage swath about 1 to 2 counties wide, with some enhanced damage from near Stevens County down through the Stearns/Kandiyohi county line. There was also a brief EF0 tornado near New London.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief tornado formed along a quasi-linear convective system (QLCS) and moved into New London. It took part of the roof off of three homes and a large shed. Other homes sustained shingle, soffit, and siding damage. At least 200 trees were broken or uprooted. Some of the adjacent damage appeared to be associated with inflow winds. The tornado was associated with a surge in the QLCS which then caused damaging downburst winds that started several miles west of New London and continued into Meeker County (see separate entries).

There was also a brief EF0 tornado near New London.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large machine shed was destroyed. Several dozen large trees and power poles were uprooted or broken. A pontoon was flipped and there was some soffit and shingle damage to a couple houses.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface temperatures reaching the mid to upper 90's combined with dew points in the 70s and lower 80s allowed for apparent temperatures, or the heat index, to exceed 105��F in rural MN counties and 100��F for multiple hours in the Twin Cities metropolitan area. Strong ridging aloft was in place over the southwestern U.S. which allowed for seasonally warm high temperatures to be carried east via zonal flow over the Central U.S. ||In the lower levels, a surface low over the Dakotas along with transport of gulf moisture from the south allowed for dew point temperatures to remain elevated the night prior to July 27th, despite cloud cover lingering from prior convection over the northern plains. As clouds cleared the next day, daytime heating increased surface temperatures in an already humid air mass, causing heat indices to reach dangerous levels. ||Some of the highest heat indices from this event were:|Granite Falls, MN in Yellow Medicine County: 116��F |Redwood Falls, MN in Redwood County: 113��F|Willmar MN, in Kandiyohi County: 111��F|Cambridge, MN in Isanti County: 108��F|St. Paul, MN in Ramsey County: 107��F|Blaine, MN in Anoka County: 107��F|Olivia, MN in Renville County: 107��F|Rush City Municipal Airport in Chisago County: 107��F|Princeton, MN in Sherburne County: 107��F|MSP Int���l Airport in Hennepin County: 103��F|Airlake Airport in Dakota County: 101��F.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Warm environmental temperatures in the mid 90s combined with high dewpoint values in the low 70s, caused high heat indices exceeding 100��F for approximately six hours. The ASOS station located at the Minneapolis International Airport recorded an apparent temperature of 103��F at 3:00 PM (CST).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms that were associated with a southward advancing cold front proceeded across Central and Southern MN and Western WI.  The meteorological setup was considered a High CAPE/Low Shear environment. Or in other words, plenty of available energy for storms to develop, but lack of turning winds with height to support rotating updrafts. Therefore, large hail was the primary threat with the line of storms that developed from Cumberland, WI down to Redwood Falls, MN and farther south.||Measured hail sizes from these storms ranged from nickel size to golf ball size hail with most falling as quarter size hailed.  |1.75 inches - Golf Ball - 5 miles NW of Owatonna, MN (Steele) |1.75 inches - Golf Ball - 5 miles E of Mankato, MN (Blue Earth)|1.50 inches - Ping Pong Ball - 1.5 miles SSE of Prior Lake, MN (Scott).

Prolonged hot and humid conditions coupled with dry mid-levels in the atmosphere, led to extreme amounts of mixed-layer convective available potential energy (MLCAPE). The existence of deep layer shear allowed for numerous strong updrafts to develop across East Central MN with the primary hazard being hail in the afternoon hours. Later that evening in Southwestern MN, a surface boundary would converge on a cold pool which then would produce a line of thunderstorms capable of producing damaging wind gusts.||Hail measurements ranged between penny (0.75 inch) to Hen-Egg (2.0 inch) size. The highest measured hail was reported in the city of Morgan, MN or 13 miles SE of Redwood Falls located in Redwood County. ||Strong winds uprooted trees, broke large tree branches across much of the region affected by these storms. The areas with the most damage occurred in Chisago County as well as a line from Nicollet to Waseca Counties. The strongest gust (57 mph) was recorded at 11:56 PM at the Mankato Regional Airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm produced a combination of quarter size hail up to half-dollar size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms that were associated with a southward advancing cold front proceeded across Central and Southern MN and Western WI.  The meteorological setup was considered a High CAPE/Low Shear environment. Or in other words, plenty of available energy for storms to develop, but lack of turning winds with height to support rotating updrafts. Therefore, large hail was the primary threat with the line of storms that developed from Cumberland, WI down to Redwood Falls, MN and farther south.||Measured hail sizes from these storms ranged from nickel size to golf ball size hail with most falling as quarter size hailed.  |1.75 inches - Golf Ball - 5 miles NW of Owatonna, MN (Steele) |1.75 inches - Golf Ball - 5 miles E of Mankato, MN (Blue Earth)|1.50 inches - Ping Pong Ball - 1.5 miles SSE of Prior Lake, MN (Scott).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the late evening of July 25, a supercell developed in the eastern Dakotas and moved into west central Minnesota. This supercell grew upscale into a small bow echo as it moved east/southeast into central and eventually eastern Minnesota during the overnight hours of the 25th into the 26th. The storm produced a damage swath about 1 to 2 counties wide, with some enhanced damage from near Stevens County down through the Stearns/Kandiyohi county line. There was also a brief EF0 tornado near New London.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Part of a roof was blown off a large machine shed. Several trees in the area were broken or uprooted.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong line of thunderstorms progressed across Nicollet County where numerous trees downed and flattened corn along a extending line from Lafayette, MN to North Mankato, MN. Local broadcast media provided a photo of a large tree branch down in Spring Lake Park which had been brought down by strong thunderstorm winds. The AWOS observation site located at Mankato Regional Airport recorded a wind gust of 50 mph at 11:56 PM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Prolonged hot and humid conditions coupled with dry mid-levels in the atmosphere, led to extreme amounts of mixed-layer convective available potential energy (MLCAPE). The existence of deep layer shear allowed for numerous strong updrafts to develop across East Central MN with the primary hazard being hail in the afternoon hours. Later that evening in Southwestern MN, a surface boundary would converge on a cold pool which then would produce a line of thunderstorms capable of producing damaging wind gusts.||Hail measurements ranged between penny (0.75 inch) to Hen-Egg (2.0 inch) size. The highest measured hail was reported in the city of Morgan, MN or 13 miles SE of Redwood Falls located in Redwood County. ||Strong winds uprooted trees, broke large tree branches across much of the region affected by these storms. The areas with the most damage occurred in Chisago County as well as a line from Nicollet to Waseca Counties. The strongest gust (57 mph) was recorded at 11:56 PM at the Mankato Regional Airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Photo of large tree branches downed by strong thunderstorm winds in Spring Lake Park. Mankato Regional Airport reported a 50 mph wind gusts at 11:56 PM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms that were associated with a southward advancing cold front proceeded across Central and Southern MN and Western WI.  The meteorological setup was considered a High CAPE/Low Shear environment. Or in other words, plenty of available energy for storms to develop, but lack of turning winds with height to support rotating updrafts. Therefore, large hail was the primary threat with the line of storms that developed from Cumberland, WI down to Redwood Falls, MN and farther south.||Measured hail sizes from these storms ranged from nickel size to golf ball size hail with most falling as quarter size hailed.  |1.75 inches - Golf Ball - 5 miles NW of Owatonna, MN (Steele) |1.75 inches - Golf Ball - 5 miles E of Mankato, MN (Blue Earth)|1.50 inches - Ping Pong Ball - 1.5 miles SSE of Prior Lake, MN (Scott).
Several supercell thunderstorms developed across the region and produce widespread hail, with occasional strong wind gusts and wind damage.

A subltle shortwave trough within northwest flow moved across the region and provided enought lift for scattered afternoon thunderstorms. There was sufficient MLCAPE (1500 J/kg) and Effective Shear (30kts) for some stronger updrafts that produced severe hail and damaging wind gusts. Overall these storms were relatively short lived and died off once the outflow undercut the updraft. |Of particular note, in Elk River a tree feel on a tent where there was a birthday party ongoing. There were 3 minor injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the late evening of July 25, a supercell developed in the eastern Dakotas and moved into west central Minnesota. This supercell grew upscale into a small bow echo as it moved east/southeast into central and eventually eastern Minnesota during the overnight hours of the 25th into the 26th. The storm produced a damage swath about 1 to 2 counties wide, with some enhanced damage from near Stevens County down through the Stearns/Kandiyohi county line. There was also a brief EF0 tornado near New London.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public works relayed a report of a tree down in Waverly.
